title: AETHRYA
label: Guides
excerpt: In a world where gods walk among mortals in mortal disguise, every stranger might be divine — and every choice you make is watched by eyes older than civilization.
text: The Veil of the Living Gods AETHRYA A Conan Exiles · 5e Text Roleplay Server In a world where gods walk among mortals in mortal disguise, every stranger might be divine — and every choice you make is watched by eyes older than civilization. ✦ Begin Your Journey → Explore the Lore 175 Lore Pages 13 Classes 47 Playable Races 30+ Deities The gods do not rule openly. They walk among you — merchant, soldier, scholar, stranger. The Veil Accord forbids divine intervention in its oldest and most absolute form. What remains is the cold war of mortal institutions, hidden agendas, and the terrible patience of immortal beings playing a very long game. What Is Aethrya? Aethrya is an immersive, narrative-driven 5e Dungeons & Dragons roleplay experience set within a Conan Exiles persistent world. Here, characters do not simply adventure through a backdrop — they inhabit a living political landscape shaped by an ancient divine cold war, where the very gods of the Faerûnian Pantheon, the Seldarine, the Morndinsamman, and beyond walk the mortal world disguised as ordinary people. The world's central mystery — and its greatest tension — is the Veil Accord : the divine treaty that forbids the gods from revealing their true nature or exercising their full power openly. Every temple, every political institution, every mercenary order and arcane college is, in some measure, a piece on the board of an invisible divine game. Players are the ones who get to look behind the curtain. 🌍 Living World A persistent Conan Exiles map where the divine cold war plays out through political events, faction conflict, and DM-driven story arcs. ⚔️ 13 Classes Full 5e class roster with deep mastery progression. Novice through Master — each tier unlocking new narrative possibilities. ✨ 30+ Deities Seven pantheons with full lore, mortal disguise identities, and player-available Pantheon Race applications. 🧝 47 Races Common, Limited, and Fabled tiers. Every race has a defined place in Aethrya's divine politics and world ecology. 🏰 7 Factions From Leilon's trade council to the Veilbreakers' investigation cells — each faction offers unique story threads. 🌀 The Weave All magic flows through Mystra's divine framework. Spell Points, mastery tiers, and a system where every cast has a hidden witness. ✦ ✦ ✦ The Three Pillars of Aethrya Divine Intrigue. The gods are here. Tessara Wand is Mystra. Aldric Brand is Tempus. Kael Ironveil is Bane. These are not distant patrons — they are characters in the world, with mortal identities, mortal vulnerabilities, and the extraordinary pressure of maintaining a disguise before creatures who are slowly, inevitably becoming more capable of seeing through it. Political Consequence. Aethrya does not hand players a stable world to adventure through. Every faction event, every power struggle, every DM arc has lasting consequence. The map changes. Institutions fall. Characters who act with genuine agency leave marks that persist through the season. Earned Mastery. The Novice → Adept → Expert → Master progression is not a time gate — it is a narrative gate. Masters are expected to embody their class's highest expression. Every tier unlocks not just mechanical improvements but access to story threads, DM events, and world-shaping capability unavailable to newer characters.

title: New Player Guide
label: Guides
excerpt: Everything you need to step through the Veil for the first time — from whitelist application to your first scene.
text: Guides New Player Guide Everything you need to step through the Veil for the first time — from whitelist application to your first scene. Getting Started Welcome to Aethrya. Before you set foot in the Veillands, there are a few essential steps. The server uses a whitelist system to ensure every player arrives prepared for the depth of roleplay Aethrya requires. This is not a barrier — it is a covenant. We want your first scene to feel earned. 📋 Step 1: Application Submit your whitelist application through our Discord. Include a brief character concept and confirm you have read the General Rules. 🎭 Step 2: Character Creation Choose your race, class, and beginning mastery tier. Common races and standard classes are available at creation. Limited and Fabled require applications. ⚙️ Step 3: Discord Setup Join the Aethrya Discord, claim your role, and introduce yourself in the welcome channel. Your staff contact will guide you to the appropriate channels. 🌍 Step 4: Enter the World Log into the Conan Exiles server and begin your first scene. Hub cities are safe starting points with active player populations. Character Creation Choices Your choices at character creation carry narrative weight. The race you select determines your relationship to Aethrya's divine politics. Your class determines which divine institutional networks you are embedded in — often without your character's knowledge. Your deity selection (for Clerics and Paladins) determines factional allegiance at the deepest level. Backstory is not cosmetic. The DM team reads every backstory and will use it to create story threads specific to your character. A Ranger who once worked in the Irondeep has already left footprints in Deepkeeper Gorrus's records. A Rogue who trained under a master of disguise may have been closer to a divine operative than either of them knew. All Common races and standard 5e classes are available at character creation. Limited races require a separate application submitted after whitelisting. Fabled races — including the Pantheon Race — require a completed standard application, written backstory, and a lore interview with the Staff team. Community Expectations Aethrya is a long-form collaborative narrative. Every scene you write is a contribution to a shared story that will be remembered. Characters grow, relationships form, consequences accrue. We ask every player to approach the experience with the generosity and care that genuine collaborative storytelling demands. This means: following through on narrative threads, respecting other players' character agency, communicating out-of-character when a scene approaches sensitive territory, and treating DM decisions as narrative collaborations rather than rulings to contest.

title: Roleplay Guide
label: Guides
excerpt: The craft of immersive, long-form collaborative storytelling — emote standards, scene conduct, consent tools, and the narrative responsibility that transforms good players into extraordinary ones.
text: Guides Roleplay Guide The craft of immersive, long-form collaborative storytelling — emote standards, scene conduct, consent tools, and the narrative responsibility that transforms good players into extraordinary ones. Emote Standards Aethrya uses third-person emote-based roleplay. Your character's name opens each post, followed by action, dialogue, or observed internal state. Every emote should advance the scene — through action, speech, environmental detail, or emotional texture that gives your scene partner something genuine to respond to. Posts that only acknowledge the previous emote without contributing forward momentum are the one form of writing this community asks you to avoid. Example Emote Serith Dawnwhisper sets her cup down with the careful deliberateness of someone who has considered whether the noise it makes is worth making. "You were at the Aeldris symposium," she says — not asking. "Tessara Wand gave the keynote." She watches for the response that arrives two seconds too slowly. Scene Conduct Godmodding — describing another character's actions or reactions without their player's consent — is prohibited. Keep your emotes to your own character's actions, leaving outcomes open to dice or negotiation. Use double brackets (( )) for OOC speech in scene channels; minimise these interruptions and relocate extended OOC discussion to designated channels. Good scene partners make their characters legible — providing behavioural and dialogue cues their partner can respond to — and remain genuinely responsive, following threads that others open rather than steering every scene toward a predetermined personal arc. The best scenes on Aethrya have come from players who brought an intention and abandoned it when something better appeared. Consent Tools The X Card applies at all times. Type X to pause any scene instantly, without explanation required. All players honour X cards immediately and without comment. For violence above standard combat threshold, romantic content, and psychological horror, pre-scene OOC confirmation is required before the scene opens — not during, not after. Narrative Responsibility Long-form roleplay requires stewardship. If your character begins a thread, follow it through. If you must step away from the server, communicate with the DM team so the threads involving your character can be managed appropriately. Consequence-embracing play — welcoming the narrative results of your choices, including the difficult and unfavourable ones — is the defining mark of Aethrya's finest storytellers. Characters who experience genuine setbacks produce richer stories than those who engineer only victories.

title: Staff Structure
label: Guides
excerpt: The team behind Aethrya — who does what, how to reach the right person, and the response standards that make the server function as a living, responsive world rather than an unattended backdrop.
text: Guides Staff Structure The team behind Aethrya — who does what, how to reach the right person, and the response standards that make the server function as a living, responsive world rather than an unattended backdrop. 🎲 Dungeon Masters Run narrative events, manage consequence arcs, adjudicate DM-led combat, play significant NPCs. The architects of Aethrya's living story. Contact: #dm-requests ⚖️ Moderators Enforce conduct standards, mediate OOC disputes, manage the whitelist review process, handle appeals. Contact: #mod-mail 📚 Lore Team Maintain setting consistency, conduct Fabled race interviews, approve Limited and Fabled applications, manage the world's canonical record. Contact: #lore-questions 🤝 Community Team Organise community events, manage social channels, coordinate new player onboarding, monitor the general health of the player community. Contact: #community-help Contact Directory Need Channel Team Response Time Rules clarification, player report #mod-mail Moderators 24–48 hours DM event request, consequence arc #dm-requests Dungeon Masters 48–72 hours Fabled application, lore interview #lore-questions Lore Team Scheduled within one week Community events, general help #community-help Community Team 24 hours Whitelist review #whitelist-applications Moderators + Lore 48–72 hours Appeals, sensitive matters #appeals Senior Staff Priority — within hours for safety concerns Do not DM staff members directly unless explicitly invited. Using the correct channel ensures your request reaches the right team and is logged appropriately. All staff channels are monitored by multiple team members — your message will not be missed.

title: Dungeon Master Guide
label: Guides
excerpt: How DM events work on Aethrya — how to request one, what to expect, and the craft of collaborative storytelling that separates a reactive encounter from a transformative narrative arc.
text: Guides Dungeon Master Guide How DM events work on Aethrya — how to request one, what to expect, and the craft of collaborative storytelling that separates a reactive encounter from a transformative narrative arc. How Events Work Aethrya's Dungeon Masters are the custodians of the world's living story. They do not script events from first scene to last — they prepare narrative frameworks, NPC rosters, and consequence trees, then follow wherever player agency leads. No prepared scene survives full contact with a player character who has decided to do something unexpected, and the DM team considers this a feature rather than a problem. Events fall into two categories. Reactive Events emerge organically from the world's ongoing storylines without requiring player initiation. Requested Events are submitted through the DM request channel, scheduled based on character mastery, request quality, and narrative fit with active arcs. ⚡ Reactive Events World-driven events triggered by ongoing faction developments, seasonal story arcs, and the accumulated weight of player choices. These arrive without announcement and reward active engagement with Aethrya's political landscape. 📜 Requested Events Player-submitted events focused on specific characters, relationships, or story threads. Include the characters involved, the narrative question you want addressed, and what outcomes you are genuinely open to. Outcomes are never guaranteed. 🌍 World Events Server-wide events with consequences affecting all players regardless of participation. These advance the season storyline and may permanently alter the world map, faction standings, or available character options. 🎯 Mastery Events Required for Adept and Master mastery transitions. The DM team presents challenges specific to your class and character arc, and assesses whether the mastery threshold has been genuinely met. Requesting an Event Submit DM event requests through #dm-requests in Discord. A strong request includes: the characters who should be present, a brief summary of the story thread you want to advance, the narrative question you want the event to engage with, and a genuine openness to outcomes you did not anticipate. Requests that specify a desired outcome in precise detail receive lower priority than requests that specify a desired question. The DM team schedules events within two weeks of approval. Good: "Vaerin has been following the lead from the mercenary camp — I want an event where she finally meets the officer who gave the order. I'm open to anything, including it going badly." Less good: "I want my character to find out who Aldric Brand really is and get proof they can share with the Veilbreakers." During an Event DM events follow standard Aethrya emote format. The DM plays NPCs and narrates environmental developments; players control only their own characters. Dice rolls are called by the DM and resolved publicly. Consequences established in a DM event are permanent unless the DM explicitly frames a consequence as reversible. Characters who die in DM events follow the standard permadeath procedure.

title: General Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: The baseline standards governing all conduct on Aethrya — the principles without which long-form collaborative roleplay cannot function, and the guarantees that make the world safe enough for genuine vulnerability in storytelling.
text: Rules General Rules The baseline standards governing all conduct on Aethrya — the principles without which long-form collaborative roleplay cannot function, and the guarantees that make the world safe enough for genuine vulnerability in storytelling. These rules apply to every player, character, and interaction without exception. They are not negotiated by in-character logic. OOC Conduct ▶ All out-of-character communication must be respectful. Disagreements about character actions are resolved through DM mediation, never OOC argument. Use (( )) to indicate OOC speech in scene channels and minimise interruptions during active scenes. The distinction between IC conflict and OOC hostility must be maintained at all times. Discrimination & Harassment ▶ Zero tolerance for real-world discrimination in OOC communication or in IC content that uses fantasy prejudice as a vehicle for real prejudice. Targeted character antagonism designed to drive a player away from the server — rather than serve genuine narrative purpose — constitutes harassment regardless of IC justification. Metagaming ▶ Characters may only act on information their character has legitimately acquired IC. OOC knowledge of divine identities, other characters' secrets, or server announcements may not inform character decisions. Divine identity metagaming — acting on OOC knowledge of a god's mortal form — is the most serious form of this violation and is treated accordingly. Consent Framework ▶ Active consent is required for scenes involving violence against player characters, romantic content, and significant psychological themes. The X Card pauses any scene instantly without explanation required. Consent given in one scene does not carry to subsequent scenes. Powergaming ▶ Prioritising mechanical advantage over narrative coherence, ignoring injuries the fiction demands, or exploiting rules against the story's internal logic is prohibited. The rules system exists to serve the narrative, not to be weaponised against it. Character Death ▶ Permadeath is possible through the death save system in DM-adjudicated combat. PvP death requires DM approval. "Permadeath without DM approval" in PvP contexts is prohibited. Death is a narrative event managed collaboratively — not an outcome imposed unilaterally.

title: Combat Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: The full Aethrya dice-based combat framework — initiative, action economy, attack resolution, spellcasting, death saves, and the PvP restrictions that ensure conflict serves the story rather than circumventing it.
text: Rules Combat Rules The full Aethrya dice-based combat framework — initiative, action economy, attack resolution, spellcasting, death saves, and the PvP restrictions that ensure conflict serves the story rather than circumventing it. Initiative & Turn Order Combat begins when a DM or affected player calls for it. All participants roll d20 + DEX modifier for initiative. The DM posts the turn order and manages the round. Players have 5 minutes to post their action; failure results in the Dodge action being taken automatically. Action Economy Action Type Count Examples Action 1 per turn Attack, Cast a Spell, Dash, Disengage, Dodge, Help, Hide, Ready Bonus Action 1 per turn (if available) Off-hand Attack, Healing Word, Cunning Action, Bardic Inspiration, Ki Reaction 1 per round Opportunity Attack, Shield, Counterspell, Sentinel, Uncanny Dodge Free Action DM discretion Brief communication, drop an item, minor environmental interaction Attacks, Crits & Death Saves Attack rolls are d20 + attack bonus versus the target's AC. Post your roll publicly alongside your emote. On a hit, roll damage dice. Critical hits (natural 20) double all damage dice; natural 1 misses unconditionally. When a character drops to 0 HP, they make death saving throws at the start of each turn: 10+ is a success, 9 or below a failure. Three successes stabilise; three failures mean death. Natural 1 counts as two failures; natural 20 restores 1 HP. Spellcasting in Combat Announce the spell name, SP cost, and targets before rolling. Concentration spells require notation; damage while concentrating demands a CON save (DC 10 or half damage taken, whichever is higher). Only one concentration spell may be maintained at a time. Player vs. Player combat requires: established IC motivation, DM notification before the conflict begins, mutual OOC acknowledgment that PvP is occurring, and full consent framework compliance. Ambush-style PvP requires DM pre-approval.

title: Building Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: The regulations governing player-owned structures, settlement plots, and the construction of permanent establishments within Aethrya's ever-shifting political landscape.
text: Rules Building Rules The regulations governing player-owned structures, settlement plots, and the construction of permanent establishments within Aethrya's ever-shifting political landscape. Plot Ownership Player characters may own one primary plot per character at Adept mastery and above. Plots are registered through the Faction Structure system — each faction controls plot allocation within their territory, and ownership implies some degree of factional affiliation even for nominally independent structures. Hub city plots are awarded by the governing faction and may be revoked if the character's conduct violates hub city regulations. Structure Placement ▶ All structures must be placed within an approved plot boundary. Encroachment on another player's registered plot without explicit OOC permission is a rules violation. Hub city structures must follow the established city aesthetic — visible inconsistencies require DM consultation. Defensive structures — walls, towers, guard posts — require factional approval one tier above basic plot ownership. Settlement Construction ▶ A settlement requires a minimum of three active player characters registered as permanent residents, a named faction sponsor or demonstrated independence charter, and DM team approval confirming the settlement's narrative fit with the current world state. Settlements that grow to five or more permanent residents receive access to the Hub Guard Rules appropriate to their tier. Abandoned Structures ▶ Structures belonging to characters inactive for 30 or more days may be reclaimed by the owning faction after staff notification. Characters who formally retire may designate an heir to inherit their plot. Structures without a designated heir after 45 days of inactivity enter the open plot registry and may be claimed by any eligible character.

title: Reroll Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: When and how a character may be retired and rerolled — including permadeath procedures, the legacy mechanics that honour extraordinary arcs, and the understanding that every ending in Aethrya is also a beginning.
text: Rules Reroll Rules When and how a character may be retired and rerolled — including permadeath procedures, the legacy mechanics that honour extraordinary arcs, and the understanding that every ending in Aethrya is also a beginning. Death in DM-adjudicated combat or confirmed narrative consequence is permanent. Permadeath is not punishment — it is the guarantee that character choices carry genuine weight. Characters who fall leave a legacy woven into Aethrya's living world. Voluntary Retirement A character may be voluntarily retired at any time. Retirement is a narrative act — the character exits the active story in a way consistent with their arc. Retired characters may reappear as NPCs at staff discretion. The player is free to create a new character immediately upon retirement approval. Retired characters at Expert or Master mastery leave a legacy record that new characters may reference in their backstory, creating thematic continuity without mechanical inheritance. Permadeath Procedure When a character reaches 0 HP in a DM event and fails their death saves — or when a DM confirms death as narrative consequence — the permadeath procedure begins. The player is notified privately by the DM before the death is confirmed publicly. Together, they discuss the death's narrative framing and legacy implications before the scene concludes. Players may create a new character immediately. New characters begin at Novice mastery and must not carry their predecessor's knowledge of secrets, contacts, or story threads unless those connections are established through public IC means. Mastery at Death Legacy Options World Impact Novice Memory only NPCs may reference the character by name Adept One legacy item Faction standing partially transferred to a designated successor Expert Legacy item + one ongoing story thread Named NPC entry; full faction standing transfer available Master Legacy item + named institution + story thread Permanent world entry; DM memorial arc available upon request

title: Class & Profession Change
label: Rules
excerpt: The narrative requirements for changing class, subclass, or profession — because in Aethrya, no transformation is purely mechanical. Every divine network connected to your original class has a response when you leave it.
text: Rules Class & Profession Change The narrative requirements for changing class, subclass, or profession — because in Aethrya, no transformation is purely mechanical. Every divine network connected to your original class has a response when you leave it. Class change is a narrative event with factional implications, not a mechanical respec. Every divine network connected to your original class has a response when you leave it. Every network connected to your new class will require demonstrated loyalty before extending full access. Class Change Requirements To change class, a character must complete documented training sessions with a character of Adept mastery or higher in the target class, satisfy a DM-approved narrative justification, and confirm with the Lore Team that the change does not contradict established character history in ways that cannot be reconciled. Current Mastery Training Sessions Required Grace Period Starting Mastery Novice 3 sessions 2 weeks Novice in new class Adept 5 sessions 4 weeks Novice in new class Expert 8 sessions + DM approval 6 weeks Novice in new class Master Story arc required — not a standard procedure DM-managed Novice in new class Subclass Change Subclass change is available once per character lifetime and requires a DM event. It is a narrative turning point, not a training-session procedure. The DM event establishes what happened to make the change inevitable — and ensures the world registers that something fundamental shifted. Oathbreaker Paladin The Oathbreaker subclass cannot be chosen at character creation. It is the consequence of a DM-adjudicated Oath violation — a Paladin who, in a meaningful narrative moment with a DM witness present, chose to break their core commitment rather than uphold it. The path back from Oathbreaker requires a full DM arc, demonstrated atonement, and at least ten documented sessions at the Oathbreaker stage before any transition is considered. It is the most demanding individual character arc on the server. Profession Change Profession change requires three documented training sessions with a master of the target profession and a 14-day grace period during which the character produces no output from either profession. Previous profession progress is archived and may not be recovered.

title: Consequence Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: How the world responds to what your character does — the living principle that every action in Aethrya carries weight, that institutional memory is long, and that a divine network's patience is not the same as its absence.
text: Rules Consequence Rules How the world responds to what your character does — the living principle that every action in Aethrya carries weight, that institutional memory is long, and that a divine network's patience is not the same as its absence. Consequences in Aethrya operate on two entirely separate levels. Narrative consequences are IC responses to character actions, managed by the DM team as story developments. Mechanical sanctions are OOC responses to rules violations, managed by the Moderation team. These systems must never be conflated — being arrested IC is not a punishment; it is the world responding to what your character chose to do, and responding to it with good RP craft is a mark of narrative maturity. Narrative Consequences Every significant IC action carries the possibility of narrative consequence. A character who commits a crime within hub city jurisdiction faces the hub guard response system. A character who makes enemies of a faction will find that faction's considerable resources redirecting toward them. A character who violates a divine institution's norms will find that institution's mortal network responding in ways both visible and — more troublingly — not. Consequence severity scales with the severity of the action, its visibility, the target's factional connections, and the character's established record. First-offense consequences for minor infractions may be as light as a formal warning. Repeat offenses, major crimes, or actions against significant NPCs escalate accordingly — and the divine networks of Aethrya have memories that span centuries. Criminal Actions & The Hub Guard ▶ Criminal actions within hub cities trigger the Hub Guard Response system immediately. Actions outside hub territories trigger faction-specific responses from any faction with jurisdictional claim over the affected region. Actions with no witnesses and no evidence trail may produce no immediate response — but Aethrya's intelligence networks are comprehensive, patient, and long-memoried. The absence of immediate consequence is not evidence that no one noticed. Divine Attention ▶ Actions that fall within a deity's domain may attract divine attention even when they do not constitute Accord violations. A character who repeatedly violates Tempus's principle of honorable engagement will find Aldric Brand's professional network intersecting their path. This is not mechanical punishment — it is a DM event hook. Divine attention is always a story opportunity, and in Aethrya, story opportunities are the most valuable currency there is.

title: Charm & Dominate Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: The consent framework governing all mind-affecting spells and abilities — a structure that honours player agency while preserving the genuine narrative power of enchantment magic in a world where Velara Dusk's counseling practice exists.
text: Rules Charm & Dominate Rules The consent framework governing all mind-affecting spells and abilities — a structure that honours player agency while preserving the genuine narrative power of enchantment magic in a world where Velara Dusk's counseling practice exists. Charm and dominate effects targeting player characters require OOC consent from the targeted player before narrative consequences are applied. Casting the spell IC proceeds normally. Applying its consequences requires an OOC conversation first. The Consent Framework Mind-affecting magic occupies uniquely sensitive territory in collaborative roleplay because it directly constrains a player's control over their own character. Aethrya's framework balances narrative realism — these spells are powerful, and that power should carry genuine story weight — with the foundational principle that no player is ever required to have their character's actions controlled by another without consent. When a mind-affecting spell succeeds mechanically, the casting player privately requests consent from the targeted player. The targeted player may consent, partially consent (defining what actions they will roleplay under the effect), or decline. If the targeted player declines, the spell still succeeded mechanically — it simply produced no observable narrative effect. The casting character does not know their spell failed to hold, which creates a narratively rich ambiguity that both players can explore. Subtle Spell Interactions ▶ Sorcerer Subtle Spell removes somatic and verbal components — meaning no other characters observe the casting. It does not remove the OOC consent requirement for applying narrative consequences. A Subtle Charm Person still requires player consent. What Subtle does affect is the evidence and investigation layer: no witness can testify to having observed spellcasting, which is exactly how a certain grief counselor in Emberreach's upper districts maintains plausible deniability. NPC Targets ▶ Mind-affecting spells targeting NPCs do not require OOC consent — the DM manages NPC responses as part of the narrative. However, charm or domination of significant NPCs (factional leaders, divine mortal-form figures, named recurring characters) requires DM involvement before and after the spell is cast, as consequences for these actions ripple through the world in ways the DM team needs to manage carefully.

title: Infiltration, Raid & Siege
label: Rules
excerpt: The structured framework for organized conflict between factions, guilds, and settlements — from solo covert operations to the full weight of a siege declaration, with all its permanent world consequences.
text: Rules Infiltration, Raid & Siege The structured framework for organized conflict between factions, guilds, and settlements — from solo covert operations to the full weight of a siege declaration, with all its permanent world consequences. Infiltration Solo or small group (1–3) covert entry for intelligence, theft, or sabotage. Requires Stealth or Deception checks, no combat declaration, no advance notice required. Evidence trail rules apply in full. Raid Organized combat assault (2–6 attackers) on a player-controlled location. Requires 24-hour declaration to the target faction's leadership. 48-hour cooldown before a second raid on the same location. Siege Large-scale assault on a settlement or fortified position. Requires 72-hour declaration, DM oversight, minimum five attackers. Resolution is a DM-managed event with permanent world consequences. Declaration Requirements Raids and Sieges require a formal declaration submitted to the Moderation team and delivered IC to the target faction's leadership. Declarations must include: the attacking group, the target location, the stated objective (what constitutes victory), and the proposed time window. Staff confirm validity before the declaration period begins. Undeclared combat against player structures is treated under the Consequence Rules as a crime rather than a structured conflict — NPC guard responses and faction reactions follow accordingly, rather than the structured resolution system. Resolution Infiltration resolution uses the skill check system. Raid resolution uses dice combat with a DM referee present. Siege resolution is a full DM event whose outcome the DM team determines based on player action, narrative context, and consequence trees prepared in advance. Siege outcomes may permanently alter faction standing, territory control, and available player options in the affected region — they are among the most consequential events Aethrya's player community can produce.

title: Tracking & Points of Interest
label: Rules
excerpt: How wilderness tracking abilities function, and how Aethrya's Points of Interest are discovered, registered, and maintained — the cartography of a living world that rewards those who wander with purpose.
text: Rules Tracking & Points of Interest How wilderness tracking abilities function, and how Aethrya's Points of Interest are discovered, registered, and maintained — the cartography of a living world that rewards those who wander with purpose. Tracking Characters with Survival proficiency or the Favored Enemy feature may track creatures or characters through wilderness regions. Tracking checks use Wisdom (Survival) against a DC set by terrain difficulty, time elapsed since the target passed, and the target's active concealment efforts. A successful check reveals the trail's direction, approximate age, and the size of the group being tracked. Characters actively concealing their trail use Dexterity (Stealth) to contest tracking attempts. Magical concealment — Pass Without Trace, Nondetection — provides specific DCs that standard tracking cannot exceed without equivalent magical assistance. A character who understands they are being tracked and who acts accordingly creates one of Aethrya's most compelling chase narratives. Points of Interest Points of Interest (POIs) are player-discovered or DM-placed locations carrying narrative significance: ancient ruins, divine artifact sites, Primordial Titan manifestation points, hidden caches, faction installations. POIs are registered with the DM team and tracked on the server's internal world map. Players who discover a POI through legitimate IC exploration may claim discovery credit — acknowledged in the world as that character's historical achievement, potentially unlocking DM event hooks tied to the location. POIs without player visits within 30 days revert to undiscovered status and may be found again by anyone. Primordial Titan manifestation sites are a special POI category under elevated DM monitoring. Characters who spend significant time at Titan sites will notice animals behaving abnormally, magical effects amplifying or dampening without cause, and some characters beginning to experience visions the DM team manages individually. Discovery of a new Titan site is among the most significant world events a player character can trigger.

title: Theft Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: What may be stolen, the evidence requirements that make a theft legitimate, the guard and faction responses that follow discovery — and the reminder that in Aethrya, every object of value has a story attached to it that extends well beyond the item itself.
text: Rules Theft Rules What may be stolen, the evidence requirements that make a theft legitimate, the guard and faction responses that follow discovery — and the reminder that in Aethrya, every object of value has a story attached to it that extends well beyond the item itself. What May Be Stolen ▶ Any item a player character possesses and has documented in their character sheet or public records may be subject to theft, subject to evidence and check requirements. Items marked as "story items" by the DM team may not be stolen without DM oversight — these are plot-relevant objects whose theft requires narrative management. Plot items, class-defining weapons registered through the DM system, and any object whose theft would constitute a real-world harm may not be stolen under any circumstances. Evidence Requirements ▶ A theft requires: a documented emote log showing the attempt, a successful Sleight of Hand check against the target's Passive Perception (or active Perception if the target has reason to be alert), and a screenshot or scene record submitted to the Moderation team within 24 hours. Theft without documentation is not recognised by the rules system, regardless of what occurred IC. Response & Recovery ▶ Theft within hub city jurisdiction triggers the hub guard investigation system — the stolen item enters a recovery registry and the victim may report to any Hub Guard NPC. Outside hub jurisdictions, recovery relies on the victim's own resources and factional connections, providing some of Aethrya's most compelling personal story hooks for those willing to pursue them. A character who was robbed and found the thief is not an unusual story here. It is the entire genre.

title: Practical Disguise Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: How disguises are constructed, registered, maintained, and penetrated — including the Veil Accord's special provisions for divine disguises that no mortal skill check was ever designed to unravel.
text: Rules Practical Disguise Rules How disguises are constructed, registered, maintained, and penetrated — including the Veil Accord's special provisions for divine disguises that no mortal skill check was ever designed to unravel. Mortal Disguises Any character may attempt a disguise using Disguise Kit tools. Quality is determined by the proficiency check result and the complexity of what is being concealed — disguising as a different race requires a higher DC than disguising within your own. Disguising as a specific named individual (Impostoring) requires the Assassin Rogue's Impostor feature or equivalent magical capability. Penetrating a disguise uses Perception or Investigation against the disguiser's Deception check. Divine Disguises ▶ Divine disguises — the mortal forms maintained by Pantheon Race players and NPC deities — operate at a categorically different level than mortal disguise work. They are sustained by divine will in ways no Deception check can replicate, and they resist Dispel Magic, which interacts only with Weave-mediated effects. Divine disguises may be penetrated by: specific magical detection devices (Galdrick Sparkwit's prototype, currently functional at forty feet), certain high-tier True Seeing applications, and — most critically — accumulated behavioural evidence. The Veilbreakers use this last method almost exclusively. Registered Aliases ▶ Characters who use disguises regularly may register recurring aliases with the DM team. A registered alias has a name, consistent visual description, and documented social role. Registered aliases are treated as persistent entities in the world's information systems — hub city records, faction rosters, wanted notices. They create a second life for your character that the world treats as real, with all the rich complexity that implies.

title: Hub Cities & Guard Rules
label: Rules
excerpt: The regulations governing neutral hub city zones — the carefully maintained armistice that makes Leilon the closest thing to safe ground in Aethrya, and the guard authority that exists only because every faction present has quietly agreed it serves their interests more than the alternative.
text: Rules Hub Cities & Guard Rules The regulations governing neutral hub city zones — the carefully maintained armistice that makes Leilon the closest thing to safe ground in Aethrya, and the guard authority that exists only because every faction present has quietly agreed it serves their interests more than the alternative. "Hub cities function because no faction finds them more useful destroyed than operational. This is the foundation of all civilised life, and it is less stable than it appears." — Compact Chair's orientation address, Leilon, every year Restricted Activities in Hub Zones Within declared hub city boundaries, the following are restricted: combat initiated without provocation, mind-affecting magic cast on non-consenting targets in public spaces, visible display of insignia from factions in active conflict with the hub compact, and construction or structural modification without plot registration approval. What is expressly permitted: passive factional affiliation, private meetings in registered premises, trade of restricted goods through licensed merchants, and personal utility magic. Guard Response Tiers Violation Severity Initial Response Escalated Response Minor — disturbance, public disruption Verbal warning; escorted removal 24-hour restriction from public spaces Moderate — assault, minor theft Detention and formal investigation Temporary ban pending resolution Severe — murder, organised crime, magical assault Full guard mobilisation; cross-faction bounty posted Permanent ban; faction-wide wanted status Veil Accord Breach Judicator notification; hub guard withdrawal DM-managed response — outside guard jurisdiction entirely

title: World Overview
label: Setting
excerpt: Six thousand years after the Divine War, the gods did not leave. They simply became quieter. Every institution you trust, every stranger who offers you something useful at exactly the right moment — in Aethrya, every coincidence has a divine address.
text: Setting World Overview Six thousand years after the Divine War, the gods did not leave. They simply became quieter. Every institution you trust, every stranger who offers you something useful at exactly the right moment — in Aethrya, every coincidence has a divine address. "No deity shall reveal their divine nature to mortal kind, nor exercise divine power in ways that mortals would classify as miraculous, under penalty of Judicator intervention. This Accord is binding upon all divine beings who operate within the mortal plane." — The Veil Accord, First Provision The World in a Single Truth Aethrya is a world where the gods lost their war, accepted a treaty, and chose to remain. Not as distant patrons issuing guidance through celestial intermediaries — as people. Walking the same streets. Eating at the same inns. Teaching at the same colleges, running the same guilds, sitting on the same courts. The Faerûnian Pantheon's Tessara Wand has been a professor at the Imperial College for thirty years. Kael Ironveil is a mid-ranking official at the Interior Ministry. Casker Morvaine is a coroner. They cannot reveal what they are. The Veil Accord prohibits it absolutely, and the Judicators enforce it with a consistency that the gods have found, over four millennia, to be entirely immune to negotiation. What remains is the cold war of mortal institutions — divine agendas pursued through proxy, patience, and the extraordinary advantage of a four-thousand-year planning horizon over the mortals who constitute their primary competition. Players are the mortals who are, gradually and through their own choices, beginning to see the shape of what surrounds them. Not because the gods want them to. Because the questions they are asking, individually and collectively, are starting to assemble into something that cannot be explained any other way. 🗺️ Geography Six major regions, each shaped by different divine hands — from the Throne of Ashes to the unmapped Veillands frontier where Primordial Titans stir beneath the stone. ✨ The Pantheons Seven divine assemblies operating through institutional proxy — each with mortal forms, hidden agendas, and four thousand years of patience. 🏰 Factions Seven player-joinable factions whose work intersects, conflicts, and overlaps in the complex pattern that Aethrya calls daily life. 📜 Season Story The current season's active plotlines — five convergence threads that are approaching a point of intersection that no single faction has fully accounted for. The Three Questions Every character in Aethrya is living inside three questions, whether they know it or not. The arc of a character's life on the server is, in one meaningful sense, the arc of their engagement with these questions. Who is walking beside you? The divine cold war is conducted through mortal institutions. Every significant NPC has a chain of authority that connects, at some remove, to a hidden god. The question of which hidden god, and what they want, is the master investigative question of the server. What does the pattern mean? The geological anomalies, the Grove Network contamination map, the coordinated animal behaviour at the frontier, the seismic events in Phandalin — these are not separate phenomena. When a character with sufficient investigation capacity assembles them correctly, the shape they form triggers the season's defining narrative event. What will you do when you know? Every faction, every divine agent, every Judicator file is ultimately waiting for the answer to this question. In Aethrya, knowledge is never its own destination. What you do with what you learn is the only thing that matters.

title: The Veillands — Geography & Regions
label: Setting
excerpt: From the imperial heartland of the Throne of Ashes to the unmapped territories where Primordial Titans stir beneath geological anomalies that only Rangers have learned to read correctly — the living map of Aethrya's world.
text: Setting The Veillands — Geography & Regions From the imperial heartland of the Throne of Ashes to the unmapped territories where Primordial Titans stir beneath geological anomalies that only Rangers have learned to read correctly — the living map of Aethrya's world. The Throne of Ashes The dominant human empire — a vast political entity spanning multiple kingdoms and city-states under a single imperial compact. Its name dates to the Divine War's conclusion, when its founding city was built upon the ash-fields of the final battlefield. Its political culture is shaped by four thousand years of divine manipulation through the Faerûnian Pantheon's competing institutions. Every succession crisis in the Throne has a divine dimension. Every major war has been orchestrated, prolonged, or concluded by at least one hidden deity working through mortal agents. Emberreach The imperial capital — seat of Throne administration, home to the Imperial College of Lore, the High Court, and a concentration of divine mortal-form agents that makes it the most politically dense city in the world. Every major power maintains a presence here. Most of those presences are disguised as something ordinary. Leilon The coastal trade hub and primary neutral port — maintained by a multi-faction compact no single power controls. The closest thing to truly neutral ground in Aethrya, and accordingly the city where every faction has a presence it insists is merely commercial. Harrowgate Border city and garrison town at the empire's northern frontier. Where Tempus's Order of the Iron Gauntlet trains officers, where Bane's Black Fist maintains a "civilian administration consultancy," and from which three military campaigns have launched in the last decade. Millhaven The gnomish engineering quarter city. Where Flan Ironpocket runs their workshop, where Galdrick Sparkwit's detection device is being fabricated, and where the intersection of divine-adjacent and artificer-class expertise has made it the most technically dangerous city in the empire. Silvenmoor & the Elven Territories Ancient elven territory stretching across the world's oldest forest systems. The Sun Elves' Preservation Circles maintain libraries of pre-Veil knowledge here — documents describing what the world looked like before gods walked in disguise. Corellon's mortal presence is felt in every grove. Rillifane's Grove Network runs through every wild space. The Circles know things that three pantheons would prefer they didn't, and their continued existence is Corellon's most meaningful ongoing defiance in the current era. The Irondeep The great dwarven underground — an interconnected system of mountain holds, deep forge-cities, and border territories where Mountain Dwarves, Gold Dwarves, and Duergar maintain their separate civilisations in careful proximity. The Morndinsamman's divine network operates here through forge-temples and clan-religious institutions shaped by Moradin's mortal presence for millennia. Durn Anvilhand's work is flawless. It has been flawless for two hundred years. The dwarves regard this as evidence of divine favour. It is evidence of something considerably more direct. Velkharra & The Underdark The Underdark dominion — Drow civilisation governed by matriarchal noble houses in perpetual competition, every house maintaining at least one surface connection its rivals do not know about. Maerath's Children operate with equal fluency in underground and surface contexts. Velkharra's politics are simultaneously the most complex and the most transparently structured in Aethrya: everyone knows the game; the stakes are simply survival. The Frontier Veillands The unmapped territories beyond the empire's frontier — where geological anomalies first indicated the Primordial Titan awakening pattern, where Rangers encounter evidence that no institutional network has catalogued, and where Phandalin operates as the furthest permanent settlement. Beyond it, the world is genuinely unknown. Every expe

title: Season Story Timeline
label: Setting
excerpt: The living record of what Aethrya's players have collectively made happen — the convergence of personal arcs, faction manoeuvres, and the slow seismic pressure of something ancient beginning to wake.
text: Setting Season Story Timeline The living record of what Aethrya's players have collectively made happen — the convergence of personal arcs, faction manoeuvres, and the slow seismic pressure of something ancient beginning to wake. Season One is currently in progress. The timeline below reflects confirmed events. Active plotlines are ongoing — their resolutions will be recorded here as they unfold, each entry a testament to the choices made by this community. Active Storylines Convergence Points The Doctoral Student — Mystra's doctoral student at the Imperial College has reached the theoretical edge of Weave-breaking research. Three divine agents are monitoring. Two player characters have independently discovered fragments of the work. Neither has yet found the other. Kael's Report — The Interior Ministry's surveillance recommendation targeting the Veilbreakers and Silent Synod was approved fourteen days ago. The operations have begun. The Veilbreakers do not yet know. One of their Research Division analysts is currently in grief counselling with Velara Dusk. The Titan Pattern — The Grove Network's contamination map has four confirmed data points. Circle-Speaker Verdant has begun corresponding with a Ranger character about the pattern. The correspondence has not yet produced the recognition that either party is on the edge of something world-altering. The Fraud Trial — Aldrus Cole's High Court proceeding is revealing Interior Ministry connections. Kael Ironveil is now personally monitoring the case. Tyr and Bane are in direct institutional conflict for the first time this season. The mortals watching the trial see a fraud case. The gods watching see a war fought with paperwork. The Blank Monk — An individual who will not confirm or deny their identity has been attending Veilbreaker public events for six weeks. The Veilbreakers' Archival Division has a file. The file contains the words "see also: Ao (unconfirmed)" and nothing else.

title: Factions of Aethrya
label: Setting
excerpt: Seven player-joinable factions whose competing agendas map directly onto the divine cold war — each offering a distinct entry point into the world's living political architecture, and each carrying threads that connect, eventually, to every other.
text: Setting Factions of Aethrya Seven player-joinable factions whose competing agendas map directly onto the divine cold war — each offering a distinct entry point into the world's living political architecture, and each carrying threads that connect, eventually, to every other. Leilon Coastal Trade City · Neutral Hub Aethrya's primary hub city — where every faction maintains a presence under the compact armistice, where Bria Goodhearth's inn has been the heart of four districts for thirty years, and where the Veilbreakers' northern chapter operates under a financial sponsor who does not yet know they are in Kael Ironveil's surveillance file. Phandalin Frontier Mining Town · Expedition Staging Ground The furthest permanent settlement — rebuilt twice, newly reporting seismic events the miners describe as non-geological, and managing an expedition ledger with seventeen entries describing the same anomalous locations in seventeen different ways. The frontier where the Titan awakening expresses itself most visibly. Southkrypt Underground City-State · Surface-Underdark Nexus Where Maerath's Children, Vhaeraun's counter-network, and a surface trading community that has deliberately chosen not to examine too carefully what it is enabling all operate in the same market district simultaneously. The most politically sophisticated environment on the server for characters who work multiple angles. The Veilbreakers Investigative Organisation · Anti-Divine Intelligence The mortal investigators building their files one careful observation at a time — currently under surveillance by a hidden god who has not decided what to do about what they know, and whose Research Division analyst is in grief counselling with Shar's mortal form. The Silent Synod Theological Research Confraternity · Forbidden Knowledge Five hundred years of continuous operation, no known founding figure, and accumulated files containing a partial Veil Accord text differing meaningfully from every publicly taught version. Their oldest open research case has been open for four hundred and eighty-seven years. Order of the Hidden Flame Paladin Order · Unknowing Divine Champions Empowered by a patron whose identity they have never determined, serving justice through an institution whose founder spent fifty years on the same question and decided conviction was sufficient. The most dramatically ironic faction in Aethrya — and the one whose patron has been waiting, with infinite patience, for a Paladin who asks the right question at the right moment. Children of the Spider Queen Maerath's Mortal Web · Intelligence Operation The most sophisticated divine intelligence operation in Aethrya — surface cells, Underdark chapters, and an organisational architecture in which no single strand can trace the shape of the whole. Their current primary objective is the Godslayer Blade. Three other factions believe it is somewhere else. The season arc will determine which is correct.

title: Languages of Aethrya
label: Systems
excerpt: Common tongues, racial scripts, divine languages, and the forbidden utterances whose very use constitutes a detectable act under certain interpretations of the Veil Accord — the linguistic architecture of a world where words have always carried more weight than they appear to.
text: Systems Languages of Aethrya Common tongues, racial scripts, divine languages, and the forbidden utterances whose very use constitutes a detectable act under certain interpretations of the Veil Accord — the linguistic architecture of a world where words have always carried more weight than they appear to. Common Languages Language Spoken By Notes Common All races (trade standard) The lingua franca of the Throne of Ashes and all hub cities Elvish Elves, Half-Elves The Long Memory is transmitted in High Elvish script; spoken dialects vary by subrace Dwarvish Dwarves Mountain and Gold Dwarf dialects are mutually intelligible; Duergar dialect is markedly distinct Halfling Halflings Primarily oral tradition — rarely written. Yondalla's protection extends most strongly to its native speakers Gnomish Gnomes, technical scholars The primary language of artificer documentation and engineering record-keeping throughout the empire Draconic Dragonborn, dragons, arcane scholars The oldest language in continuous use — predates the Veil Accord. Certain spells exist recorded only in Draconic Undercommon Drow, Duergar, Underdark traders The trade pidgin of the deep places; carrying it on the surface marks a speaker as Underdark-affiliated Orc Orcs, Half-Orcs, frontier peoples Associated with Gruumsh's ancient tradition; the language of Barbarian oral war-histories Divine Scripts Celestial The script of the upper planes and divine bureaucracy. Required for Cleric and Paladin divine communication above Adept tier. Naturally readable by Aasimar. Infernal The script of the Nine Hells — used in Tiefling heritage documents, Warlock Fiend pact language, and Bane's Black Fist contract fine print. The provisions no one reads carefully enough. Abyssal The chaos-language of the Abyss. Associated with Maerath's demon-adjacent operations and certain Great Old One Warlock communications. Audibly disturbing when spoken aloud in enclosed spaces. Primordial The language of elemental forces — spoken natively by Genasi as inherited dialects. When used at Titan sites, it has effects that the DM team manages on a per-character basis. Use with deliberate caution. Certain languages are classified as Forbidden Tongues under the Imperial Accord Faith's theological doctrine. Their use in public is prohibited; their ritual use attracts Judicator attention. Deep Speech — the language of Aberrations and certain Primordial entities — is the most frequently encountered forbidden tongue in Aethrya's current season arc, as its speakers increasingly overlap with characters investigating the Titan awakening pattern. The Judicators have not publicly addressed what it means that more people seem to be learning it.

title: Magic & The Weave
label: Systems
excerpt: Every arcane spell cast in Aethrya is drawn through a living divine framework maintained by a hidden goddess. Every divine spell is answered by a patron who is present in the mortal world, somewhere, in mortal form. Magic in Aethrya is never private. There is always someone on the other end.
text: Systems Magic & The Weave Every arcane spell cast in Aethrya is drawn through a living divine framework maintained by a hidden goddess. Every divine spell is answered by a patron who is present in the mortal world, somewhere, in mortal form. Magic in Aethrya is never private. There is always someone on the other end. Mystra is the Weave. The Church of the Seven Stars' registration system is not merely administrative — it is Tessara Wand's comprehensive census of every arcane talent in the Throne of Ashes. You are in her files. The question is which section. The Spell Point System Aethrya uses Spell Points rather than traditional spell slots. A character's SP pool is determined by their class at each mastery tier, recovering fully on Long Rest. Wizards also restore SP on Short Rest via Arcane Recovery; Warlocks recover all SP on Short Rest. Spell Tier Level Equiv. Mastery Required SP Cost Cantrip / Fluff Cantrips Any spellcaster 0 SP Novice 1st–2nd level Novice 1–2 SP Adept 3rd–4th level Adept 2–3 SP Expert 5th–6th level Expert 4–5 SP Master 7th–9th level Master 5–7 SP Arcane vs. Divine Magic Arcane magic — Wizard, Sorcerer, Artificer — flows through the Weave. It is subject to the Weave's structural conditions and registered with Mystra's institutional census. Divine magic — Cleric, Paladin, Druid, Ranger — flows through the direct connection between mortal and deity. It is not Weave-dependent, but it is never truly private: the deity on the other end of the connection is always present, always aware, and always accumulating information about what their mortal network is doing. The doctoral student at the Imperial College has, through research into arcane theory, approached the theoretical foundations of Weave-breaking — a discovery that would disrupt both systems simultaneously. Mystra has not yet decided what to do. Shar's representative was assigned to monitor the situation two weeks ago. Three other divine agents are watching the same student. The student is attending a seminar on theoretical arcane constants and has no idea. Concentration Concentration spells — spells that require maintained mental focus — end immediately if the caster casts another concentration spell or falls unconscious. Taking damage while concentrating requires a CON saving throw (DC 10 or half the damage dealt, whichever is higher) to maintain concentration. A character may never concentrate on more than one spell simultaneously. Managing concentration in complex encounters is one of Aethrya's most meaningful tactical challenges and one of the clearest expressions of the difference between a Novice and an Expert spellcaster under pressure.

title: Professions Overview
label: Systems
excerpt: Every character requires a profession — the economic and social role that grounds them in Aethrya's living world beyond their class's combat and magical capabilities. Professions have their own mastery track, drive the player economy, and determine which institutional networks value your non-combat contributions.
text: Systems Professions Overview Every character requires a profession — the economic and social role that grounds them in Aethrya's living world beyond their class's combat and magical capabilities. Professions have their own mastery track, drive the player economy, and determine which institutional networks value your non-combat contributions. Professions are not secondary to class — they are the part of a character's life that classes do not cover. A Master Wizard who cannot sustain themselves economically is dependent on factional patronage. A Novice Fighter with Master-level Blacksmith skills has resources, institutional connections, and economic leverage that their class alone cannot provide. The two tracks advance independently, and the combination of class mastery and profession mastery defines a character's full capability range more completely than either track alone. Blacksmith Weapons, armour, tools. The forge tradition of Moradin's Irondeep — producing the equipment that every other profession and class ultimately depends upon. Alchemist Potions, poisons, reagents. The server's most consumed profession output — healing supplies, buff consumables, and experimental compounds at Master tier. Merchant Trade, appraisal, commerce. From market stall to cross-faction economic diplomacy — Vergadain's professional domain expressed through mortal enterprise. Scholar Research, history, investigation. The most dangerous profession in a world where collective memory has been managed by hidden gods for four thousand years. Hunter Tracking, trapping, harvesting. The frontier's primary early-warning system for the Titan awakening pattern — if the character knows what to look for. Quartermaster Logistics, bulk supply, faction operations. The economic leverage that comes from controlling what flows where — and the ability to redirect it. Prospector Geological surveying, mineral assessment. First to document the subsonic vibration patterns at Titan sites — before anyone knew what they were measuring. Woodsman Lumber, forestry, territorial knowledge. Grove Network adjacent — their working territory overlaps directly with the contamination mapping operation. Forager Botanical gathering, wilderness survival. Herbalist-adjacent — documenting the accelerated growth and die-off cycles that mark Titan proximity. Cartographer Map-making, territorial documentation. The profession that turns investigation into permanence — and whose work the Veilbreakers' Field Division uses more than any other profession's output.

title: The Wound System
label: Systems
excerpt: How wounds are applied, tracked, treated — and why a character who survived a fight with three grievous injuries tells a richer story than one who emerged unscathed. Physical conflict in Aethrya leaves marks, and those marks are part of the narrative.
text: Systems The Wound System How wounds are applied, tracked, treated — and why a character who survived a fight with three grievous injuries tells a richer story than one who emerged unscathed. Physical conflict in Aethrya leaves marks, and those marks are part of the narrative. The wound system exists to ensure combat carries consequences beyond Spell Point expenditure. A character who survives a difficult fight is not the same character who entered it. Wounds are not punishments — they are the body's record of what the character has been through, and they make every subsequent interaction richer for carrying that history. How Wounds Are Applied Wounds are applied when a character drops to 0 HP and is revived, takes damage from a critical hit above a threshold scaled to the character's HP maximum, or is targeted by a wound-inflicting ability, trap, or environmental hazard. Not every combat produces wounds — only consequential ones. DM events have DM confirmation; player-versus-player combat wounds require Moderation confirmation. Wound Severity Application Trigger Mechanical Effect Recovery Minor 0 HP and revived; minor trap –1 to affected ability checks Long rest + basic healing Moderate 0 HP twice in same encounter; significant critical hit –2 to related checks; disadvantage on strenuous activity 3 days rest or magical treatment Severe 0 HP three times; major combat trauma –4 to related checks; temporary ability score reduction 1 week rest + healer treatment + DM confirmation Grievous DM-adjudicated catastrophic event Permanent until magically cured; DM-managed individual effects Specific magical treatment — DM event required Wound Roleplay Active wounds must be reflected in your character's emotes and scene behaviour. A character with a severe shoulder wound performing overhead strikes without acknowledging the limitation is not engaging with the system in good faith. Wounds are narrative assets — they give other characters legitimate hooks for care, concern, or tactical observation, and they make every scene in which they appear more textured than it would be without them. The Monk's Wholeness of Body feature, the Cleric Life domain's healing capabilities, and the Artificer Alchemist's Restorative Reagents all interact specifically with the wound system in ways that give these options distinct value beyond raw HP numbers — a meaningful reward for characters whose builds support recovery as well as conflict.

title: Leilon — The Neutral Shore
label: Factions
excerpt: The coastal trade city where every faction maintains a presence and no faction holds dominance — a carefully tended armistice that functions as the server's primary starting hub and the closest approximation of neutral ground in a world defined by hidden allegiances.
text: Factions Leilon — The Neutral Shore The coastal trade city where every faction maintains a presence and no faction holds dominance — a carefully tended armistice that functions as the server's primary starting hub and the closest approximation of neutral ground in a world defined by hidden allegiances. "Leilon is neutral. This is not a natural state. It is maintained by continuous effort, mutual distrust, and the shared recognition that a city where nobody wins is preferable to a battlefield where everyone loses." — Hub Compact Charter, founding article City Districts The Harbour Quarter Leilon's economic engine — the docks, warehouses, counting houses, and merchant guilds that process trade flows justifying the city's neutrality. Every major faction maintains a merchant presence. The consequential meetings happen in the back offices, after business hours, with the shutters drawn. The Council District The governing compact's administrative heart. Seven council seats, one per major trading faction. The eighth seat — the Compact Chair — rotates annually. Currently held by a merchant whose patron is known to exactly three people in the city, none of them on the Council. Temple Row Multiple divine institutions operating in close proximity under the compact's religious tolerance charter. Tessara Wand's Seven Stars chapter house is here. So is Casker Morvaine's death registry office. So is a modest House of Many Chances annex whose card tables are never empty. The Scholars' Ward An informal academic district grown up around a well-funded library whose restricted collection has been accessed by six individuals in the last decade whose identities match no registered researcher credential. The librarian maintains a file. The file is now forty pages long. Notable Figures Compact Chair Mira Crestfall — Current Chair, shipping merchant, and financial supporter of the Veilbreakers' northern chapter. Does not know this connection is already in Kael Ironveil's surveillance file. Archivist Peldon — Head of the library's restricted collections, who has begun corresponding with the Silent Synod about patterns in his forty-page file of unauthorised access incidents. Bria Goodhearth — Halfling innkeeper, community pillar, beloved figure to half the city. Yondalla's mortal form. The faction that threatens her establishment will discover, too late, how many favours Bria has accumulated over thirty years of quiet, genuine hospitality. Guard Commander Hessa Ironwall — Hub Guard Commander, seven years in post — a record. Attributes her tenure to being genuinely afraid of no single faction. Whether this is wisdom or bravado is a standing debate among her deputies.

title: Phandalin — The Frontier's Edge
label: Factions
excerpt: The furthest permanent settlement into the unmapped Veillands — rebuilt twice, fortified against a threat it has never fully been able to name, and increasingly aware that the geological reports its expedition parties bring back describe something no existing model can explain.
text: Factions Phandalin — The Frontier's Edge The furthest permanent settlement into the unmapped Veillands — rebuilt twice, fortified against a threat it has never fully been able to name, and increasingly aware that the geological reports its expedition parties bring back describe something no existing model can explain. Overview Phandalin has been destroyed and rebuilt twice in the last two centuries. It endures because the Wave Echo Cave system at its centre — source of the ore that has funded every reconstruction — is too valuable to abandon. The current settlement is the most fortified iteration yet: stone-walled, approximately four hundred permanent residents, with a rotating population of expedition personnel, researchers, and the particular breed of fortune-hunter who prefers honest frontier odds to Leilon's calculated ones. Phandalin operates under partial hub status — its central market district follows hub city conduct rules, while the rest of the town and all territory beyond its walls follows standard frontier rules. This creates a distinctive social gradient: civilised norms from the market square inward, increasingly flexible standards as you approach the gate, and beyond the gate, whatever the wilderness chooses to be that day. The Tresendar Estate The town's administrative centre, repurposed from an old manor. Town Master Harlan Wester maintains the expedition ledger here — the official record of every party that has gone into the Veillands and what they reported back. The ledger has seventeen entries describing the same anomalous locations in seventeen different ways. Wave Echo Cave The mine that justifies the town. Currently at 60% capacity following a seismic event that collapsed two lower shafts three months ago. The miners who were working those sections say the stone moved in ways that were not geological. Wester has written this down. He has not yet decided who to tell. The Stonehill Inn Phandalin's largest public house and the de facto information exchange. Expedition parties debrief here over drinks that are stronger than they appear. The board near the door holds an ever-changing collection of maps, wanted notices, and commission requests from people who prefer not to give their names. The Shrine of Luck A modest but immaculately maintained Tymoran shrine. Its halfling priest Linene Graywind has sent at least a dozen expedition parties into the Veillands with a sermon on bold action. Three of them came back with extraordinary findings. She counts this as evidence. Kessa Brightfall has visited twice.

title: Southkrypt — The In-Between City
label: Factions
excerpt: Where the Underdark and the surface world negotiate their terms — a city built in the political gap between two civilisations, trading in discretion as readily as goods, and offering every character who arrives there a connection they did not expect and cannot fully explain.
text: Factions Southkrypt — The In-Between City Where the Underdark and the surface world negotiate their terms — a city built in the political gap between two civilisations, trading in discretion as readily as goods, and offering every character who arrives there a connection they did not expect and cannot fully explain. Overview Southkrypt exists in the space between — the uppermost Underdark passages where surface and underground trade routes converge into a market that neither world would openly claim. Its population is genuinely mixed in ways that no surface city matches: Drow who chose commerce over matriarchal politics, Deep Gnomes pursuing technical independence from clan authority, Duergar merchants operating outside sanctioned guild structures, and a substantial surface contingent who find that Southkrypt's combination of underground discretion and surface access is extremely useful for certain categories of business that benefit from no single jurisdiction knowing the whole of it. Governance here is by Merchant Council, whose actual authority shifts with whoever controls the most critical trade routes at any given moment — making Southkrypt's politics notably more fluid than any surface city's institutionalised power structures. This fluidity is exhausting for traditional diplomats and ideal for individuals who prefer leverage to allegiance. Both types are present in significant numbers. Factions Within the City Maerath's Children (Southkrypt Chapter) — The most sophisticated surface-facing operation in the Children's network, running through three legitimate trading houses, a financial services firm, and what the Merchant Council has officially declined to investigate too carefully. Their intelligence reach here is the most comprehensive in the Underdark-adjacent world. Vhaeraun's Masked Lord Cult — An underground movement among the city's Drow male population, providing financial networks, escape assistance for those seeking surface life, and the particular satisfaction of undermining the matriarchal structures that made surface life seem worth seeking. Their operation and Maerath's overlap in exactly the ways both parties find intolerable. The Merchant Council — Seven seats, four currently held by figures whose other affiliations would surprise at least three of their fellow Council members. The chair's position rotates quarterly, a system designed to prevent consolidation that is working precisely as intended and producing exactly as much chaos as that implies. Southkrypt offers Aethrya's most politically complex beginning. A character who arrives here is immediately embedded in three overlapping intelligence operations, two divine networks, and a merchant economy that rewards information as generously as goods. Every relationship you form in Southkrypt has a relationship you don't yet know about. This is not a hazard — it is the city's defining character.

title: The Veilbreakers
label: Factions
excerpt: The mortal investigators who have dedicated their lives to exposing hidden divine activity — operating in isolated cells, building their files one careful observation at a time, and currently being surveilled by a hidden god who has not yet decided what to do about what they know.
text: Factions The Veilbreakers The mortal investigators who have dedicated their lives to exposing hidden divine activity — operating in isolated cells, building their files one careful observation at a time, and currently being surveilled by a hidden god who has not yet decided what to do about what they know. Membership in the Veilbreakers makes you a target of every divine faction simultaneously. Kael Ironveil's surveillance operation began fourteen days ago. The Veilbreakers do not yet know. One of their Research Division analysts is currently in grief counselling with Velara Dusk. Organisation Structure The Veilbreakers operate in cells — small groups sharing only the information necessary for their specific operational scope. No single cell knows the full roster of the organisation. This structure was designed to prevent the infiltration that destroyed the predecessor organisation, the Accord Watchers, forty years ago. It works. The gods find it deeply, genuinely irritating. Research Division Document analysis, historical pattern-matching, theological inconsistency identification. The most intellectually demanding cell type and the most valuable. The doctoral student investigation began when a Research analyst noticed curriculum theorems predating their supposed discovery by two hundred years. Field Division Surveillance, evidence collection, contact cultivation. The operatives who follow the leads that Research identifies. Their methods overlap extensively with Rogue and Ranger class capabilities — not coincidentally. Archival Division Maintains the Veilbreaker files — dossiers on every suspected divine mortal-form identity. The Faerûnian file is the largest. The entry on Tessara Wand contains six independently sourced observations that she has not aged in thirty years of faculty service. It is marked "Unconfirmed." Communications Cross-cell information routing using coded channels rotating on a 7-day cycle. The organisation's greatest structural vulnerability: it requires central coordination that necessarily creates a map. The map is exactly what Kael Ironveil wants.
