What They Do Not Yet Know The Veilbreakers do not yet know: that Kael Ironveil's surveillance operations began fourteen days ago, that one of their Research analysts is in active therapy with Shar's mortal form, that the Blank Monk who has attended their last six public events is not a prospective recruit, or that their most significant open case — the Veil Accord's founding document anomaly — has already been independently identified by the Silent Synod and cross-referenced with evidence that makes it considerably more alarming than the Veilbreakers currently believe.

title: The Silent Synod
label: Factions
excerpt: The secretive theological research confraternity that knows more about the Veil Accord than any mortal institution should — and whose founding documents contain a preface attributed to someone who was present at the Accord's signing and has not yet decided what to do about what they know.
text: Factions The Silent Synod The secretive theological research confraternity that knows more about the Veil Accord than any mortal institution should — and whose founding documents contain a preface attributed to someone who was present at the Accord's signing and has not yet decided what to do about what they know. Overview The Silent Synod operates as a scholarly network — theologians, historians, and philosophers sharing research through a private publication system with no public registry, no institutional address, and no membership list any outside investigation has successfully obtained. Members know each other by assigned designations. Their accumulated research represents the most comprehensive mortal understanding of divine presence in Aethrya's history. No one knows how the organisation was founded. The earliest records suggest approximately five hundred years of continuous operation. No founding figure has ever been identified. The founding documents' preface is attributed to "the Individual who will not be named, who was present at Aeldris, and who has not yet decided what to do about what they know." Every member who reads it forms a different theory about who wrote it. The Synod's oldest open research case is an attempt to answer this question. It has been open for four hundred and eighty-seven years. What the Synod Holds The Silent Synod's accumulated files contain: a partial text of the Veil Accord differing meaningfully from the version taught in any theological institution; a list of forty-seven individuals across six centuries believed to be divine mortal-form agents, with thirty-one matched to specific deities; a theoretical framework suggesting the Judicators are not Ao's instruments but something considerably older; and a paper on the relationship between Primordial Titan emergence patterns and divine cold war escalation cycles — written forty years ago, whose predictions have been accurate to within a seasonal margin of error. Synod membership is by invitation only, extended after observation of the candidate's research work — which the candidate generally does not know is being evaluated. Invitations arrive anonymously. The path to membership requires demonstrating, through sustained IC research and investigation, the kind of theologically sophisticated thinking that Synod observers consider worth cultivating. This is the most intellectually demanding faction entry in Aethrya, and the one that asks the most interesting questions of the characters who pursue it.

title: Order of the Hidden Flame
label: Factions
excerpt: The Paladin order that serves divine justice without knowing which hidden god empowers them — whose founder spent fifty years trying to identify her patron and concluded the answer didn't matter. The god on the other end of their power agrees. For reasons neither party has fully disclosed.
text: Factions Order of the Hidden Flame The Paladin order that serves divine justice without knowing which hidden god empowers them — whose founder spent fifty years trying to identify her patron and concluded the answer didn't matter. The god on the other end of their power agrees. For reasons neither party has fully disclosed. Overview The Order of the Hidden Flame was founded approximately two hundred years ago by Serevah Goldmantle, a Paladin whose oath-empowerment significantly exceeded what any known patron relationship should provide. She spent fifty years attempting to identify her patron — found only silence, and an enduring, reliable supply of divine power. She founded the Order on the theological principle that power granted without identification is the purest form of divine endorsement: the god who empowers you is the one who finds your principles worth supporting, full stop, regardless of institutional affiliation. The Order does not affiliate with any single deity's church. Its members are Paladins of various Oath traditions united by a justice orientation operating outside factional allegiance. Their patron — whose identity the DM team has determined and prepared extensive content around — finds that Paladins not trying to advance any faction's agenda are, for specific divine strategic reasons, exactly the agents needed in Aethrya's current political climate. The irony of this arrangement is total and, from the patron's perspective, intentional. Current Operations Leilon Compact Arbitration — Representing parties who cannot afford factional advocates in the Compact's commercial dispute process. Three of those parties have connections to divine network operations that the Order has not yet identified. The Death Pattern Investigation — Parallel to the Veilbreakers' investigation of Casker Morvaine's three-century records, working from the legal angle. Neither the Order nor the Veilbreakers are yet aware of the overlap. Northern Legion Command Irregularities — Legal support for a case revealing command chain anomalies, working alongside Aldric Brand's military assessment from the opposite direction. Also not yet aware of the overlap.

title: Children of the Spider Queen
label: Factions
excerpt: Maerath's mortal web — surface cells, Underdark chapters, and the most sophisticated intelligence operation any deity runs in the Veil era. Every transaction in their network is connected to something else. No strand knows the whole shape of what it supports.
text: Factions Children of the Spider Queen Maerath's mortal web — surface cells, Underdark chapters, and the most sophisticated intelligence operation any deity runs in the Veil era. Every transaction in their network is connected to something else. No strand knows the whole shape of what it supports. Overview The Children are organised as a spider's web — a central node connected to peripheral cells by lines no single cell can trace to their full extent. Maerath's mortal form does not directly command the Children; she manages the web's architecture, ensuring information flowing through it serves her purposes without any strand knowing what shape the whole makes. Membership ranges from true believers in Maerath's theological vision to mercenary contractors who know only their specific assignment and their handler's designation. The organisation works because it does not need its members to understand it — only to do their part. The Godslayer Blade The Children's current primary operational focus is an artifact of the Divine War era whose theological implications are significant enough that multiple divine factions maintain standing operations to locate it. The Children believe it is in the eastern Veillands, based on a pattern of evidence accumulated over thirty years. Three other factions believe it is somewhere else. At least one of those factions is correct. The Children are methodical enough that their location might still be right. The season arc will determine which. Surface Operations Leilon — A trading house specialising in rare textile imports. Its back-room conversations are considerably more interesting than the inventory suggests. Emberreach — A financial consulting firm whose client list overlaps with the Interior Ministry's surveillance targets in a way that neither party has yet noticed. Southkrypt — Three simultaneous legitimate businesses, two of which the Merchant Council has officially declined to investigate. The third is the interesting one. Surface cell members generally do not know they serve Maerath directly. They know they work for an organisation that values discretion, pays reliably, and makes requests that always connect to something significant about six months after they fulfilled them. The discovery of who they actually serve — available as a DM arc at Adept mastery — is one of the most carefully designed narrative revelations in Aethrya. It is not a surprise that ends a story. It is a surprise that begins one.

title: Classes Overview
label: Classes
excerpt: Thirteen classes — each deeply embedded in Aethrya's divine politics in ways that are not always visible at character creation. Your class does not merely describe how you fight or cast. It describes which divine network you were born adjacent to, and what that network wants from you.
text: Classes Classes Overview Thirteen classes — each deeply embedded in Aethrya's divine politics in ways that are not always visible at character creation. Your class does not merely describe how you fight or cast. It describes which divine network you were born adjacent to, and what that network wants from you. In Aethrya, class choice has institutional consequences. A Wizard is registered with Tessara Wand's Church of the Seven Stars the moment their talent manifests. A Cleric's training is provided by a divine institution whose ultimate leadership answers to a hidden god. A Paladin's Oath channels power from a source they may never have formally identified. Even a Rogue — ostensibly the least institutionally entangled class — operates in an information economy that every divine network actively cultivates and monitors. ⚔️ Fighter The martial professional — serving Tempus's Order of the Iron Gauntlet, Bane's Black Fist military branches, or operating as an independent contractor whose skills every faction covets. ✨ Wizard Systematic arcane mastery — every spell drawn through Mystra's living Weave, every caster in Tessara Wand's registration files, every advanced thesis under three divine agents' simultaneous monitoring. ⛪ Cleric The divine conduit — directly embedded in a hidden god's institutional infrastructure, whether they know it or not. Divine Intervention at Expert is a DM ticket to your patron's mortal-form network. 🛡️ Paladin Oath-bound divine warrior — power flowing through commitment to principles rather than a named patron, which is exactly the kind of theological loophole a careful god designed four thousand years ago. 🗡️ Rogue The information professional — Expertise, Reliable Talent, and the Impostor feature that approaches divine disguise quality. Every faction recruits heavily from this tradition for exactly the same reasons. 🌲 Ranger The wilderness expert — first to encounter Titan awakening evidence, essential to the Grove Network's contamination mapping, and positioned at the threshold of the season's most significant revelation. 🎵 Bard The information economy — Bardic Inspiration, the broadest skill platform, and the awareness that Erevan Ilesere's mortal form is listening to what stories are being told right now. 🌿 Druid Pre-institutional nature connection — independent of every divine network's formal structure, which makes Druids both uniquely free and uniquely valuable to the Grove Network's ongoing work. 🌀 Warlock The negotiated contract — power through agreement with an entity whose full nature you may never determine. In Aethrya, the entities available for such agreements include hidden gods. 🥋 Monk Internal cultivation — no obvious divine sponsor, which every faction finds suspicious in exactly the ways that make a Monk genuinely interesting to sit across a table from. 💫 Sorcerer Innate power — Metamagic, Subtle Spell, and the Divine Soul's theological anomaly that Mystra and Shar have both written papers about without sharing their findings with each other. ⚙️ Artificer Engineering as magic — Infusions, the Spell-Storing Item, and the workshop mentorship of two hidden gods whose detection device is the season's most strategically consequential object. 🔥 Barbarian Primal force — Rage accessing power the Veil Accord was never written to govern, and the Zealot thread that runs directly into the Primordial Titan awakening arc. All classes use the Novice → Adept → Expert → Master progression. Each tier unlocks new mechanical capabilities, new story threads, and new visibility — at Master, you are a world-shaping figure who cannot easily move through Aethrya unobserved. See Mastery Progression for the full advancement requirements.

title: Mastery Progression
label: Classes
excerpt: The four-tier advancement system that governs every class and profession in Aethrya — not a time gate but a narrative gate. Each tier is a commitment to inhabiting your character's capabilities at their fullest expression before accessing the next level of possibility.
text: Classes Mastery Progression The four-tier advancement system that governs every class and profession in Aethrya — not a time gate but a narrative gate. Each tier is a commitment to inhabiting your character's capabilities at their fullest expression before accessing the next level of possibility. Novice Entry The foundation. Character creation tier. Basic class features, starting spell access, foundational faction relationships. Where every story begins. Adept Tier 2 Demonstrable competence. Subclass features, expanded spell tiers, deeper faction integration. Requires 3 attended DM sessions and DM team assessment of roleplay quality. Expert Tier 3 Class mastery — capabilities that reshape encounters and investigations. Requires participation in a major narrative arc and DM confirmation that Expert-tier behaviour has been demonstrated. Master Tier 4 World-shaping capability. The highest expression of your class. DM interview, documented mentorship of Novice characters, and the understanding that Master characters cannot move unnoticed. Advancement Requirements Tier Sessions Required Narrative Requirement Staff Involvement Adept 3 DM sessions attended Established factional role; documented character arc progress DM team review Expert 5 DM sessions (cumulative) Participation in a major arc; demonstrated class-specific excellence DM assessment + Lore Team confirmation Master 8 DM sessions (cumulative) Major world contribution; mentorship of 2+ Novice characters documented DM interview + Senior Staff approval What Mastery Really Means Mastery in Aethrya is not a reward for time spent. It is a recognition that the character has earned their capabilities through demonstrated excellence in the roleplay that those capabilities require. A Novice Cleric who has attended three sessions and been present does not automatically advance to Adept — they advance when the DM team's observation of their roleplay confirms that they understand what being an Adept Cleric means, both mechanically and narratively. This standard exists because of the world Aethrya is. When an Expert character uses Divine Intervention, the world responds through DM event architecture. When a Master character acts, every divine faction that monitors that character class has a protocol for it. The mastery system is the guarantee that these world-shaping capabilities are in the hands of players who understand the responsibility they carry — and who will use them to make the world's story richer rather than simply more complicated.

title: Fighter
label: Classes
excerpt: The martial professional whose skill sustains every divine faction's ground operations — serving Tempus's Order of the Iron Gauntlet, Bane's Black Fist military branches, or operating as an independent contractor whose capabilities are courted by every power that needs a capable sword and cannot afford to ask where its loyalties currently lie.
text: Classes Fighter The martial professional whose skill sustains every divine faction's ground operations — serving Tempus's Order of the Iron Gauntlet, Bane's Black Fist military branches, or operating as an independent contractor whose capabilities are courted by every power that needs a capable sword and cannot afford to ask where its loyalties currently lie. Fighter No Casting STR or DEX Primary All Armor Martial Versatility Key Features: Action Surge · Second Wind · Extra Attack (up to 3) · Indomitable · Combat Superiority (Battlemaster) Martial Archetypes Battle Master Champion Eldritch Knight Samurai Cavalier Psi Warrior Rune Knight Arcane Archer Setting Role Action Surge — an additional full action once per short rest — makes the Fighter Aethrya's highest single-round damage output option. Extra Attack scales from two attacks at Adept to three at Expert, giving each mastery tier a meaningful combat capability increase. Indomitable at Expert allows rerolling failed saves, addressing the Fighter's primary vulnerability to spell effects. The Battle Master's Superiority Dice system is the most tactically flexible Fighter option — Maneuvers like Riposte, Menacing Attack, and Commander's Strike translate into social and narrative capability as well as combat advantage, making the Battle Master the most narratively versatile Fighter build. The Order of the Iron Gauntlet trains all commissioned officers in Battle Master techniques, and Aldric Brand's personal assessment of promising officers is known to reflect Battle Master capability more than raw combat power. Fighters serving the Order of the Iron Gauntlet access Tempus's professional military network — intelligence on current conflicts, access to military-grade equipment through official channels, and Aldric Brand's direct attention at Expert mastery. Fighters serving the Black Fist access Bane's institutional efficiency and the Interior Ministry's administrative reach. Fighters serving neither discover that truly independent skilled professionals are the rarest and most coveted commodity in Aethrya's current political climate.

text: Classes Wizard Every spell you cast is drawn through the living framework of a hidden goddess's domain. The Church of the Seven Stars' registration of your arcane talent is Tessara Wand's personal intelligence census. The doctoral thesis your advisor praised last month has three divine agents monitoring its theoretical direction. Wizard Full Arcane INT Primary No Armor Weave-Dependent Key Features: Arcane Recovery · Spell Mastery (Expert) · Signature Spell (Master) · Ritual Casting Arcane Traditions Abjuration Conjuration Divination Enchantment Evocation Illusion Necromancy Transmutation Bladesinging War Magic Setting Role & Divine Connections Wizards are the systematic arcane casters of Aethryan civilisation — their power derived from study, theoretical understanding, and deliberate mastery of Mystra's Weave. The Church of the Seven Stars' registration system is not merely administrative; it is Tessara Wand's comprehensive census of every arcane talent in the Throne of Ashes. Every Wizard on the server is in Mystra's files. Some more prominently than others — and those prominent files are shared with parties the Wizard has not consented to. The doctoral student currently working under Tessara Wand has reached the theoretical edge of Weave-breaking research. Any Wizard who pursues advanced theoretical arcane work at Expert mastery or above will encounter this research — and the multiple divine agents monitoring its direction. Whether that encounter becomes an alliance, a conflict, or something stranger depends entirely on the choices the character makes when the monitoring becomes visible. Abjuration: The Arcane Ward feature creates passive protection scaling with mastery — the single most valuable survivability feature for a low-armour caster in extended combat. At Master tier, Spell Resistance grants advantage on saves against spells, making the Abjurer the most resilient pure-arcane option on the server. Illusion: Illusory Reality at Master tier allows one illusion per short rest to become real for one minute — the most narratively flexible Master-tier ability available for investigation and disguise work. Necromancy: Kelemvor's mortal-form network has an active file on every Necromancy Wizard above Novice mastery in the Throne of Ashes. This is not incidental. Necromancer characters who reach Adept mastery will receive a DM event hook tied to Casker Morvaine's three-century death pattern investigation.

They always answer in ways that maintain plausible deniability.

The institutions themselves are genuine; the services they provide are real; the communities they build are meaningful. Domain Institution Hidden Principal Arcana Church of the Seven Stars Tessara Wand (Mystra) War Order of the Iron Gauntlet Aldric Brand (Tempus) Order / Tyranny The Black Fist Kael Ironveil (Bane) Justice Order of the Even Hand Aldrus Cole (Tyr) Life The Harvest Fellowship Maren Seedhallow (Chauntea) Grave / Death The Doomguide Order Casker Morvaine (Kelemvor) Protection The Vigilant Order Aldric Steadfast (Helm) Moon / Navigation Temple of the Moonmaiden Reva Silverpath (Selûne) At Expert mastery, Divine Intervention functions as a DM ticket — a formal narrative request invoking your deity's mortal-form network in the character's vicinity. No divine agent will confirm what they are. This is the Accord at work.

title: Paladin
label: Classes
excerpt: Your power flows through an Oath to principles, not allegiance to a patron — the divine workaround for a world where gods cannot openly champion mortal warriors. The god who answers your Oath may not be the one you assumed. Your conviction, not your assumption, is the point.
text: Classes Paladin Your power flows through an Oath to principles, not allegiance to a patron — the divine workaround for a world where gods cannot openly champion mortal warriors. The god who answers your Oath may not be the one you assumed. Your conviction, not your assumption, is the point. Paladin Half Divine/Martial CHA Primary All Armor Oath-Bound Key Features: Lay on Hands · Divine Smite · Aura of Protection · Aura of Courage · Sacred Weapon Sacred Oaths Devotion Ancients Vengeance Conquest Redemption Glory Watchers Oathbreaker ✦ Setting Role & The Aura The Aura of Protection — which extends Charisma modifier as a bonus to all saving throws for the Paladin and nearby allies — is one of Aethrya's most significant passive benefits in group play. It makes the Paladin a genuine force multiplier in any scene requiring resilience. Divine Smite allows additional radiant damage on any melee weapon hit at the cost of Spell Points, creating the server's highest burst-damage melee ceiling when combined with critical hits. The Order of the Hidden Flame's members are Aethrya's most dramatically positioned Paladins — empowered by a patron whose identity they cannot determine, serving justice through an institution whose founder spent fifty years on the same question and decided conviction was sufficient. Any Paladin character who seeks their order's counsel will find themselves welcomed into that tradition of principled uncertainty, which the DM team considers one of the richer ongoing arcs available to the class. The Oathbreaker subclass is unavailable at character creation. It is the consequence of a DM-adjudicated Oath violation in a meaningful narrative moment — a Paladin who chose, with a DM witness present, to break their core commitment rather than uphold it. The path back is the most demanding RP arc on the server: a full DM story arc, demonstrated atonement consistent with the new Oath's principles, and at least ten documented sessions at the Oathbreaker stage before any transition is considered. It is not a story that begins easily. It is a story worth telling.

title: Rogue
label: Classes
excerpt: In a world built on hidden agendas and carefully constructed ignorance, your skill set is not merely useful — it is the primary challenge every divine network must solve before it can operate safely. The Veilbreakers recruit heavily from your tradition. So does the Children of the Spider Queen. Both know why.

Rogue No Casting DEX Primary Light Armor Intelligence-Driven Key Features: Sneak Attack (1d6→5d6) · Expertise · Evasion · Uncanny Dodge · Reliable Talent (Master) Roguish Archetypes Thief Arcane Trickster Assassin Inquisitive Mastermind Phantom Scout Soulknife Swashbuckler Setting Role & The Impostor Problem Rogues possess a natural relationship to every faction operating in the shadows — which, in Aethrya, is every faction. Expertise doubles proficiency bonus in chosen skills, creating the server's highest ceiling for Stealth, Deception, Perception, and Investigation checks. Reliable Talent at Master mastery treats any roll below 10 as a 10 — making a Master Rogue's expertise skills essentially incapable of catastrophic failure. The Assassin's Impostor feature — studying a creature and replicating their appearance, speech, and mannerisms well enough to pass mundane inspection — is one of the few mortal capabilities that approaches divine disguise quality. The Judicators reviewed two incidents in the last century where an Assassin Impostor was mistaken for a divine mortal-form disguise by investigators specifically looking for one. The Judicators declined to intervene, for reasons that remain on record as "insufficient cause established." The Veilbreakers have noted this in their file on the Judicators.

title: Ranger
label: Classes
excerpt: The wilderness professionals who encounter Primordial Titan evidence first, who read the frontier's anomalies before any institution has catalogued them, and who stand at the threshold of the season's most significant revelation — if they recognise what the pattern means before it recognises them.
text: Classes Ranger The wilderness professionals who encounter Primordial Titan evidence first, who read the frontier's anomalies before any institution has catalogued them, and who stand at the threshold of the season's most significant revelation — if they recognise what the pattern means before it recognises them. Ranger Half Divine/Nature WIS Primary Medium Armor Frontier Specialist Key Features: Hunter's Mark · Favored Enemy · Natural Explorer · Extra Attack · Tireless Ranger Archetypes Beast Master Hunter Gloom Stalker Horizon Walker Monster Slayer Fey Wanderer Swarmkeeper The Titan Awakening Arc Rangers are Aethrya's primary access point to the season's most consequential unfolding storyline. The contamination pattern — accelerated growth and die-off cycles, magnetic anomalies, coordinated animal territorial withdrawal, the subsonic vibration pattern — is visible first to those whose professional domain is the wilderness. A Ranger who participates in Grove Network operations at Expert mastery will begin receiving Circle-Speaker Verdant's reports. The contamination map has four data points. When it has sufficient points to form a complete picture, the shape it forms is not geological. It is intentional. The Beast Master's Primal Companion functions as an ecological early-warning system near Titan sites — animals respond to Titan proximity in ways no magical instrument currently replicates, making a companion animal the most sensitive detection device available for the season's most significant developing threat. The Bond mechanic, which allows the Companion to act on your turn, creates one of Aethrya's most tactically flexible and narratively resonant partnerships.

title: Bard
label: Classes
excerpt: The information economy of Aethryan civilisation — the profession that every divine network simultaneously cultivates and fears. In a world where four thousand years of divine manipulation has required meticulous management of collective memory, the Bard who asks the wrong questions in the right venue is more dangerous than the Paladin who draws a sword.

Bard Full Arcane/Social CHA Primary Light Armor Broadest Skill Platform Key Features: Bardic Inspiration (d6→d12) · Jack of All Trades · Expertise · Song of Rest · Countercharm Bard Colleges Lore Valor Glamour Swords Whispers Creation Eloquence Spirits Setting Role & Erevan's Watch Bardic Inspiration — the bonus action grant of a die to an ally's attack roll, ability check, or saving throw — is the defining group-play contribution of the Bard class: a gift that scales with mastery from d6 to d12, representing the accumulation of genuine craft. Jack of All Trades adds half proficiency to all untrained checks, creating the broadest skill floor on the server. These features together make a Bard the character most likely to contribute meaningfully in every scene type, regardless of what that scene requires. Erevan Ilesere's current mortal form — the street performer Merry Tanglewood — operates at a level of social and performance capability mechanically equivalent to a Master Bard with College of Eloquence. Any Bard character who shares performance space with Merry will find their craft assessed by someone whose judgment about what stories deserve to be told — and to whom, and when — has been shaping mortal information culture for four thousand years. This is not a encounter that will leave the character unchanged. The DM team has prepared extensively for what it produces.

title: Druid

Druid Full Nature/Divine WIS Primary Non-Metal Armor Primordial Awareness Key Features: Wild Shape · Timeless Body · Beast Spells · Ritual Casting · Primal Strike Druid Circles Land Moon Dreams Shepherd Spores Stars Wildfire Twilight Wild Shape as Investigation Tool Wild Shape is Aethrya's most versatile intelligence-gathering capability — a Druid in eagle form observing a private meeting from above, or in rat form moving through a guarded warehouse, possesses access to spaces and perspectives that no other class can match without significant magical investment. At Circle of the Moon, Wild Shape combat forms scale meaningfully from Novice through to Master, providing a second mode of engagement that grows more formidable with each tier of experience. The contamination pattern that Circle-Speaker Verdant is documenting — the accelerated growth and die-off cycles that mark Primordial Titan proximity — becomes visible to Druid characters participating in Grove Network operations at Expert mastery and above. The data points form a shape. When the shape is complete and identified, the Druid who makes that identification will trigger a server-wide DM response that advances the season storyline in ways no other class can access through the same origin point. This is the Druid's most significant contribution to the world's unfolding story — and the Grove Network is patient enough to wait for the right character to find it.

title: Warlock
label: Classes
excerpt: Your power comes from a negotiated agreement. In a world where the most powerful entities available for such arrangements include hidden gods operating in mortal disguise, the question of who you actually bargained with carries implications that grow more urgent at every mastery tier. What does your patron want? The answer changes when the Titan pattern becomes visible.

Warlock Pact Magic CHA Primary Light Armor Short Rest Recovery Key Features: Eldritch Blast · Eldritch Invocations · Pact Boon · Mystic Arcanum · Agonizing Blast Otherworldly Patrons Fiend Great Old One Archfey Undying Celestial Hexblade Genie Undead Fathomless The Patron Arc & The Titans Warlocks sustain consistent magical output where Wizards and Sorcerers would be depleted — Short Rest Pact Spell recovery combined with the Eldritch Invocation customisation system creates the server's most adaptable sustained caster. Invocations selected at Adept mastery and expandable at Expert and Master are retrain-able once per tier, allowing the Warlock's capabilities to evolve with their patron relationship rather than calcify at character creation. The Great Old One patron occupies a uniquely resonant position in Aethrya's cosmological structure. The Primordial Titans — especially the Waking One and the Seventh — are the closest mortal-world parallel to the alien, ancient, incomprehensible patron archetype. A Great Old One Warlock whose patron's identity connects to the Titan awakening is not merely a compelling backstory concept — it is a documented in-world possibility the DM team has developed extensive content for. The question of what your patron wants from you, as the Titan pattern becomes clearer and the season's pressure builds, is the most significant and most carefully prepared character arc available to this class.

title: Monk
label: Classes

Monk No Casting WIS/DEX Primary No Armor (Unarmored) Ki-Driven Key Features: Ki Points · Martial Arts · Flurry of Blows · Stunning Strike · Evasion · Stillness of Mind Monastic Traditions Open Hand Shadow Four Elements Mercy Astral Self Ascendant Dragon Long Death Sun Soul Shadow Monks & Shar's Domain Ki points — refreshing on Short Rest — power the Monk's distinctive action economy: Flurry of Blows delivering two additional unarmed strikes as a Bonus Action, Patient Defense converting a Bonus Action to the Dodge action, Step of the Wind enabling Dash or Disengage without consuming a full action. Stunning Strike at Adept mastery imposes one of the server's most powerful control conditions on failed CON saves. At Master mastery, Empty Body grants hour-long Invisibility and resistance to all damage except force — the single most powerful defensive state available to any non-magical class in Aethrya. Shadow Monks occupy particularly compelling theological territory. Their Shadow Step teleportation functions through the same magical darkness that Shar claims as divine domain; their Shadow Arts cantrips include Darkness, Pass Without Trace, Silence, and Darkvision. Whether this overlap is coincidental, reflects ancient connections that predate Shar's current theological framework, or indicates active divine interest in a class whose independence makes it a uniquely valuable asset is a question the lore team has answered — and prepared to deliver through a DM arc available to Shadow Monk characters at Expert mastery. The answer is not what anyone expects.

title: Sorcerer
label: Classes
excerpt: Your magic flows from within — from bloodline, from cosmic circumstance, from proximity to forces whose scale you may never fully comprehend. Mystra and Shar have both written research papers on what it means that divine power can bleed into mortal bloodlines through centuries of proximity to hidden gods. Neither has published their findings. Both have noted your name.

Sorcerer Full Arcane (Innate) CHA Primary No Armor Metamagic Specialist Key Features: Sorcery Points · Metamagic · Font of Magic · Flexible Casting · Innate Spellcasting Sorcerous Origins Aberrant Mind Divine Soul Draconic Bloodline Shadow Magic Storm Sorcery Wild Magic Metamagic & Subtle Power The Metamagic system — Twin, Empower, Quicken, Extend, Heighten, or Subtle — creates Aethrya's most creatively flexible magical toolkit. No other class can modify spells in these ways, and this capacity for adaptation makes Sorcerers the most inventive casters available in scenarios that reward improvisation over preparation. Subtle Spell, which removes somatic and verbal components from a spell's execution, makes a cast completely invisible as spellcasting to any observer. This carries profound implications for the Charm and Dominate Rules consent framework, for investigation mechanics, and for the particular operating style of Velara Dusk's counselling practice — which the DM team does not draw attention to at character creation, but which Sorcerer characters discover at Adept mastery with a specificity that is not coincidental. The Divine Soul subclass is theologically anomalous in Aethrya's framework — a character channelling divine power without belonging to any deity's institutional structure. Whether this origin represents bloodline accident, deliberate divine operation outside traceable religious networks, or evidence of divine power leaking into mortal heritage through centuries of proximity to hidden gods is the central question of this subclass's Expert-tier story arc. Both Mystra and Shar have theories. The character's arc is the evidence that determines which theory is correct.

An Artificer who seeks mentorship from either enters the most productive workshop — and the most closely watched — available in Aethrya.

Artificer Half Arcane INT Primary All Armor Infusion Specialist Key Features: Infusions · Magical Tinkering · The Right Tool · Spell-Storing Item (Expert) · Arcane Firearm Artificer Specialists Alchemist Armorer Artillerist Battle Smith Setting Role & the Gnomish Tradition Artificers operate where Garl Glittergold's Clockwork Ceremony — the gnomish theological tradition of sacred engineering — meets Mystra's Imperial College network. The Infusion system allows creation of semi-permanent magical enhancements to mundane objects, producing a sustained supply of magical items that no other class can maintain. At Expert mastery, the Spell-Storing Item allows a non-spellcaster to cast a stored spell without magical ability — a mechanical transfer of divine or arcane capability that faction supply systems across Aethrya actively seek to contract for. Galdrick Sparkwit's divine-presence detection device — currently functional at forty feet, being extended with Flan Ironpocket's fabrication expertise — is the season's defining Artificer-class engineering achievement. The device's strategic implications are understood by every deity in Aethrya. None of them have formally moved to stop it. An Artificer character who investigates, assists, or builds upon this research will find themselves at the centre of the most significant technological development in Aethrya's current story arc, with every divine faction watching their progress with careful, unacknowledged attention. Master Artificers are expected to produce original magical research or devices, document a significant application of their craft in a DM event, and demonstrate the engineering discipline that distinguishes genuine Artificer mastery from mere magical item accumulation. The bar is the bar that Galdrick and Flan set by example — which is extraordinarily high, because both of them are gods.

Barbarian No Casting STR Primary No Armor (Unarmored) Rage-Driven Key Features: Rage · Reckless Attack · Danger Sense · Brutal Critical · Relentless Rage · Persistent Rage Primal Paths Berserker Totem Warrior Ancestral Guardian Storm Herald Zealot Beast Wild Magic Setting Role & The Primordial Thread Rage is Aethrya's most powerful sustained combat state — providing a damage bonus, resistance to physical damage, advantage on Strength checks and saves, and immunity to the charmed and frightened conditions. Reckless Attack offers the server's highest-risk, highest-reward baseline attack option: advantage on all attack rolls for a round at the cost of giving attackers advantage against you. The combination of these features makes Barbarians capable of combat output that no other class matches at consistent deployment. The Zealot Barbarian's divine Rage damage, the Ancestral Guardian's protective ancestral spirits, the Totem Warrior's spirit guides — these mechanics invoke divine-adjacent forces without belonging to any recognised pantheon's institutional framework. The Veil Accord was constructed to govern gods operating in mortal form; it was not constructed to address mortals who access primordial power through non-institutional channels. This legal gap has never been formally adjudicated by the Judicators. A Zealot Barbarian who investigates the divine source of their Rage will find that it traces to the primordial war-force that powered the Primordial Titans' direct engagement with mortal civilisation during the Age of Revelation — and that the Titan awakening unfolding in the current season flows through the Zealot's class mechanics in ways that no other class can access through the same origin. This investigation thread is the most unique and most narratively consequential character arc the Barbarian class offers.

title: Races of Aethrya
label: Races
excerpt: Forty-seven playable races across three availability tiers — each carrying their own cultural history, divine entanglements, and established role within the world's political and ecological structure. Your race determines your initial relationship to Aethrya's divine politics before your first scene opens.
text: Races Races of Aethrya Forty-seven playable races across three availability tiers — each carrying their own cultural history, divine entanglements, and established role within the world's political and ecological structure. Your race determines your initial relationship to Aethrya's divine politics before your first scene opens. 🧑 Common Races 13 races available at standard whitelist. Humans, dwarves, elves, halflings, gnomes, and more — each embedded in at least one divine network's operational strategy from the moment they appear in the world. 🌙 Limited Races 7 races available in restricted server-wide quantities. Drow, Avariel, Fey'ri, Satyr, Hexblood, Fox Hengeyokai, Yuan-Ti Pureblood. Elevated narrative visibility; post-whitelist application required. ✨ Fabled Races 4 races requiring full Fabled application — Pantheon Race, Cambion, Mind Flayer, True Vampire. The most demanding and most rewarding character options available. Full application process required. Race Tier Philosophy The three tiers reflect narrative weight as much as mechanical complexity. Common races are the world's baseline — characters who function naturally within its social structures and whose presence in any setting raises no immediate flags. Limited races are visible departures from that baseline — a Drow in Leilon's market, an Avariel in Emberreach, prompts reactions from every character who encounters them, and those reactions are part of the story. Fabled races exist at a scale of narrative consequence where every scene they enter is shaped by what they represent — and none more so than the Pantheon Race, which represents a hidden god in mortal form. The application requirements for each tier exist to match the investment they ask of the DM team with the investment they require of the player. A Fabled race character has a Lore Team contact, a prepared story arc, and DM-managed ongoing consequences that no other character type receives automatically. The application process is the covenant that makes that investment sustainable.

title: Fabled Race Expectations
label: Races
excerpt: The elevated standards, behavioural commitments, and community responsibilities that define the Fabled designation — not as restrictions imposed upon you, but as the architecture that transforms the most extraordinary character options into genuine narrative forces within the living world.
text: Races Fabled Race Expectations The elevated standards, behavioural commitments, and community responsibilities that define the Fabled designation — not as restrictions imposed upon you, but as the architecture that transforms the most extraordinary character options into genuine narrative forces within the living world. Fabled races are Aethrya's highest-tier character options. They are extraordinary precisely because they carry extraordinary responsibility. Staff review every application, conduct a lore interview, and monitor Fabled characters actively throughout their arc. These are not limitations — they are the covenant that makes Fabled characters worth becoming. The Elevated Standard Fabled race players are held to a higher standard across every dimension of their server conduct — not because the rules are different, but because the visibility and narrative significance of their characters means their conduct shapes the experience of every player who interacts with them. A Fabled race character who breaks rules or engages inconsistently with their race's lore damages not only their own arc but the world's coherence for everyone around them. Fabled characters are expected to: maintain race-specific behavioural guidelines in every scene; engage constructively with DM-initiated story threads within a reasonable timeframe; uphold server conduct rules at a standard that sets the tone for the community rather than satisfying the minimum; and communicate proactively with the Lore Team when their arc encounters challenges. These expectations exist because Fabled characters are pillars of the world's story architecture, and pillars are worth building carefully. Revocation Conditions Fabled status may be revoked by the Lore Team following documented violations of Fabled-specific conduct requirements. Revocation is not a punishment — it is a determination that the designated character concept is not being executed in a way that serves the server's narrative. The player is not banned; they may reapply after a 60-day reflection period. Application Process Step 1 — Foundation. Complete the standard whitelist and establish a non-Fabled character at Novice mastery. Demonstrate that you understand the world before asking to be extraordinary within it. Step 2 — Application. Submit a written backstory of at least 600 words, a description of your intended arc direction, and a statement of how your Fabled character's presence will serve the server's narrative rather than solely your own story. Step 3 — Interview. A 30–45 minute voice conversation with the Lore Team covering your chosen race's lore, character concept, and understanding of the elevated expectations. This is a conversation, not an examination — the goal is to ensure you and the team are building the same character together. Step 4 — Onboarding. Upon approval, you receive a Fabled briefing document, your designated Lore Team contact, and your character's initial DM arc seed — the first thread of a story that is already waiting for you.

text: Races Common Races The thirteen races available as standard whitelist options — each carrying their own cultural history, divine entanglements, and place within Aethrya's living world. Every common race is embedded in at least one divine network's operational strategy, whether or not their players are yet aware of it. Human The most numerous mortal race — adaptable, politically dominant in the Throne of Ashes, and the primary target of every Faerûnian Pantheon institution's influence operations. Every major divine agenda is expressed primarily through human institutional structures. Humans are, in a meaningful sense, the medium through which the divine cold war is conducted. Mountain Dwarf The warrior-caste dwarves of the upper Irondeep — stoic, honour-bound, deeply loyal to clan and Moradin. Their forge-temple tradition sustains the most intimate mortal-divine connection in Aethrya. Durn Anvilhand's professional assessments carry weight in every Mountain Dwarf hold, whether or not anyone present understands why. Gold Dwarf The trade-focused dwarves of the Deep Holds — masterful craftspeople and cautious of outsiders in the particular way that comes from having seen four thousand years of outsiders prove that caution correct. Vergadain's commercial intelligence network runs through Gold Dwarf merchant families in ways that the families themselves generally regard as exceptional business acumen. Moon Elf The most socially engaged of elven subraces — curious, traveller-inclined, and deeply tied to the Long Memory tradition transmitted through Aelindra's worship. Their cultural instinct toward genuine connection with other peoples makes them naturally effective in the intelligence-gathering operations that multiple divine factions rely on. This is not a coincidence. Wood Elf Forest-dwelling elves of the Veillands border regions — agile, reclusive, and the primary practitioners of Rillifane's Grove tradition. Their territorial knowledge of the frontier regions makes them the most valuable characters for the early stages of the Titan awakening investigation. What they have been seeing at the edges of their territory for the last three years is in the Grove Network's files. Sun Elf The aristocratic elves of ancient Silvenmoor — proud keepers of pre-Veil knowledge, closely aligned with Corellon's Preservation Circles. Their Long Memory spans the full four thousand years of the Veil era. A Sun Elf Scholar character who commits to the Preservation Circles' research is working with documents that no other race's institutional tradition has maintained. Half-Elf Children of human-elven unions — caught between two Long Memories, prized as diplomats and intelligence operatives by factions that require fluency in both cultural frameworks. Every major power maintains Half-Elf figures in positions that require navigating the boundary between human political structures and elven institutional depth. Halfling Small, resilient, community-focused wanderers — quietly protected by Yondalla's accumulated goodwill network and profoundly underestimated by every faction that has ever moved against a halfling community. The cost of that underestimation is documented in the Veilbreakers' oldest case files and nowhere else. Gnome Forest Gnome variant — fey-touched illusionists and naturalists with an instinctive theological curiosity that makes them natural, if typically unwitting, investigators of divine activity. The gnomish tradition of taking things apart to understand how they work does not stop at mechanical devices. Garl Glittergold's mortal form represents where this instinct leads when it has four thousand years and divine patience to work with. Dragonborn Draconic-heritage warriors whose ancient compact with chromatic and metallic dragons predates the Veil Accord and gives them a unique position in divine politics: an existing non-human institutional framework that the Accord's provisions were not designed to fully accommodate. Limited races carry elevated narrative visibility: a Drow in Leilon, a Hengeyokai in the Throne of Ashes, are noticed in ways a human or elf is not, and that visibility is both a story asset and a story challenge.
text: Races Limited Races Races available in restricted quantities — their scarcity within the server's population reflecting their scarcity within the world itself. Limited races carry elevated narrative visibility: a Drow in Leilon, a Hengeyokai in the Throne of Ashes, are noticed in ways a human or elf is not, and that visibility is both a story asset and a story challenge. Drow The Sundered elves of the Underdark — matriarchal, transactional, deeply entangled with Maerath's web. Among the most politically sophisticated races in Aethrya, and the one whose surface presence creates the most immediate and complex reaction from every faction they encounter. Avariel Winged elves of the high peaks — extremely rare, associated with Aerdrie Faenya's Windrunner network, and granted flight as a racial trait. One of the three races in Aethrya that can genuinely surveil from above without magical augmentation. Every intelligence network has considered what to do about this. Most have concluded the answer is "recruit." Fey'ri Sun Elf/fiend bloodline — a limited population carrying the mark of ancient infernal corruption alongside elven grace. Their demographic rarity means each Fey'ri character carries significant individual narrative weight; their existence provokes theological questions that the divine networks have not reached consensus on. Satyr Fey-blooded goat-like humanoids from the Feywild — resistant to magic, innately musical, and drawn into Aethrya through planar thinning events that the Veil Accord's provisions did not anticipate. Their magic resistance creates interesting complications for every faction that routinely uses Charm and Dominate effects as operational tools. Hexblood Mortals marked by hag magic or Feywild corruption — bearing distinctive features, Darkvision, and an involuntary connection to the fey courts. Their existence is a visible reminder that the divine cold war's collateral effects extend into bloodlines, and that some of those effects are inherited by descendants who had no part in the original transaction. Fox Hengeyokai Shapeshifting fox-spirit beings of eastern traditions — capable of human and fox form, associated with trickery, illusion, and divine messenger traditions that predate the Faerûnian Pantheon's presence in this region. Their institutional memory of what the world looked like before the Veil is limited but genuine. Pureblood Yuan-Ti The most human-appearing of the serpent-folk — nearly indistinguishable from humans to casual observation, immune to poison, and deeply enmeshed in Maerath's intelligence operations. Their ability to present as human in all surface contexts makes them among the most practically useful assets in any faction requiring seamless urban infiltration. Limited race slots are tracked server-wide to maintain appropriate population balance. Current availability is posted in the #whitelist-status channel on Discord. If a desired limited race is currently at capacity, you may join the waitlist — slot openings typically occur within two to four weeks as characters complete arcs and retire.

title: Fabled Races
label: Races
excerpt: The most extraordinary character options in Aethrya — including the Pantheon Race, which allows a player to embody a hidden god themselves. Each Fabled race represents a categorical shift in the kind of story a character tells and the weight of responsibility that character carries within the world.
text: Races Fabled Races The most extraordinary character options in Aethrya — including the Pantheon Race, which allows a player to embody a hidden god themselves. Each Fabled race represents a categorical shift in the kind of story a character tells and the weight of responsibility that character carries within the world. Pantheon Race ✦ Become one of Aethrya's hidden gods — walk in mortal form under the Veil Accord, maintain your disguise through any challenge, accumulate divine power through Mastery tiers, and shape the world's story from the inside of the most carefully constructed narrative position on the server. The highest-demand Fabled option and the most demanding to play well. Cambion The mortal children of fiend and humanoid unions — bearing multiple fiendish features, carrying the mark of infernal heritage in their appearance and capabilities, and navigating a world that has very strong reactions to both. Their arc is defined by the question of what heritage means when the bearer had no voice in acquiring it. Mind Flayer Illithid — psionic aberrations of the deep places, ancient enemies of the Gith races, and the most cognitively alien playable character in Aethrya. A Mind Flayer character among mortals is a continuous exercise in inhabiting a consciousness that experiences the world through fundamentally different sensory and relational frameworks. The most demanding Fabled role for sustained, consistent characterisation. True Vampire Full vampiric transformation — ageless, capable of creating Fledgeling vampires, associated with Vorthax's undead intelligence network, and under continuous Judicator monitoring. The only Fabled race that begins its arc already known to a specific divine agent, creating an immediate and inescapable divine entanglement from the first scene. All Fabled races require the full application process described in the Fabled Race Expectations page — written backstory, Lore Team interview, and onboarding documentation. Applications are reviewed weekly. See #fabled-applications in Discord for current wait times and submission requirements.

title: Pantheons of Aethrya
label: Pantheons
excerpt: Seven divine assemblies maintaining mortal operations across the known world — from the Faerûnian Pantheon's institutional empire to the Primordial Titans who predate every theology that claims to explain them. The divine cold war is not between good and evil. It is between agendas that have had four thousand years to become sophisticated.
text: Pantheons Pantheons of Aethrya Seven divine assemblies maintaining mortal operations across the known world — from the Faerûnian Pantheon's institutional empire to the Primordial Titans who predate every theology that claims to explain them. The divine cold war is not between good and evil. It is between agendas that have had four thousand years to become sophisticated. 👑 Faerûnian Pantheon The dominant human pantheon — a coalition of sovereign deities operating through competing institutions that collectively constitute the Throne of Ashes' entire governing apparatus. Mystra, Tempus, Bane, Tyr, Shar, and more — every one of them present, disguised, and working. 🌟 The Seldarine The elven divine court — managing the Long Memory tradition, the Preservation Circles' dangerous archives, and the Windrunner intelligence network that moves faster than any ground-based courier service. 🕷️ Dark Seldarine The Drow divine assembly — Maerath's web, Vhaeraun's counter-network, Eilistraee's genuine redemption path. The most internally fractured pantheon and the most comprehensively surveilled. ⚒️ Morndinsamman The dwarven divine family — Moradin's forge-temples, Vergadain's commercial intelligence, Dumathoin's geological silence in the face of a Titan awakening that falls squarely within his domain. 🌾 Yondalla's Children The halfling divine family — whose strategy of accumulated genuine goodwill has produced a network that every faction underestimates until the moment they try to move against it. ⚙️ Lords of the Golden Hills The gnomish divine family — who have produced the season's most consequential technological development: a device that detects divine presence. Operational. In mortal hands. Every deity in Aethrya has formed an opinion. 🌋 Primordial Titans Ancient beyond any theological framework — not bound by the Veil Accord, not addressed by it, and currently expressing themselves through the season's defining geological mystery. Three of them. One still unconfirmed. All divine operations in Aethrya are conducted under the Veil Accord — the treaty that forbids open divine revelation and is enforced by the Judicators. Four thousand years of compliance have made subtlety so habitual that some gods have forgotten what operating without it feels like. Others have not forgotten, and are watching the Titan awakening with the particular attention of beings who remember what happened the last time something this large moved without the Accord's provisions applying.

title: The Pantheon Race
label: Pantheons
excerpt: The most extraordinary character option in Aethrya — the ability to play a hidden god in mortal form. Subject to the Veil Accord's full constraints, the Judicators' ongoing monitoring, and the extraordinary dramatic pressure of maintaining a perfect disguise before a world that is slowly becoming more capable of seeing through it.
text: Pantheons The Pantheon Race The most extraordinary character option in Aethrya — the ability to play a hidden god in mortal form. Subject to the Veil Accord's full constraints, the Judicators' ongoing monitoring, and the extraordinary dramatic pressure of maintaining a perfect disguise before a world that is slowly becoming more capable of seeing through it. The Pantheon Race requires the complete Fabled application process: written backstory, Lore Team interview, designated contact, and onboarding arc documentation. It is the highest-demand Fabled option and the one the Lore Team evaluates most carefully. The quality of the application determines the depth of the arc prepared for the character. The Veil Accord's Constraints A Pantheon Race character is a deity operating under the Veil Accord's full provisions. Their divine power is real — but its expression is constrained to what can pass as mortal capability. They may not perform miracles. They may not reveal their divine nature. They may not exercise power in ways that would be classified as miraculous by an observant mortal. The Judicators monitor known divine mortal-form operations continuously. Any Accord violation triggers a Judicator response within 24 hours. What remains is still extraordinary. A Pantheon Race character has access to divine perception — the awareness of their domain's activities across the world. They maintain connections to their divine institutional network. They have the patience and planning horizon of an immortal being. They are, in every scene they occupy, the most strategically sophisticated individual present — even when they are performing the role of an ordinary person with perfect conviction. Mastery — God Mastery Tiers Pantheon Race characters use the standard Mastery system but with content specific to their divine portfolio. Each tier unlocks additional access to divine perception, institutional network depth, and carefully constrained expressions of divine capability that remain within Accord limits. Master tier — the highest — places a Pantheon Race character at the threshold of Accord-limit capabilities. What they choose to do at that edge is the defining question of their arc. Mastery Divine Access Constraint Level Novice Basic domain awareness; mortal-level capabilities Full Accord provisions apply Adept Institutional network access; domain-adjacent perception within 1 mile Miracle threshold strictly enforced Expert Expanded domain expression; divine communication with portfolio entities Judicator monitoring intensifies — any Accord adjacency triggers review Master Near-Accord-limit capabilities; direct network command; world-shaping domain expressions within constraints Every significant action reviewed by Judicators. The Accord's edge is the character's permanent address. The Pantheon Race's central dramatic question is not "how much power can I use" — it is "how long can I maintain this?" A god in mortal form is playing a role that cannot slip. Not in a crisis. Not under pressure. Not when someone you care about is at risk and you could simply stop all of this if you would just be what you actually are. The Veil Accord is not a mechanical limit. It is a four-thousand-year commitment to never breaking character, and the most compelling Pantheon Race arcs are the ones where maintaining it costs the character something real.

title: The Faerûnian Pantheon — Full Roster
label: Pantheons
excerpt: Not a unified church but a coalition of sovereign deities with overlapping domains, individual theological traditions, and a four-thousand-year history of rivalry conducted through the most elaborate system of institutional proxies ever assembled. Every Faerûnian institution you trust is managed by a being you have never seen in their true form.
text: Pantheons The Faerûnian Pantheon — Full Roster Not a unified church but a coalition of sovereign deities with overlapping domains, individual theological traditions, and a four-thousand-year history of rivalry conducted through the most elaborate system of institutional proxies ever assembled. Every Faerûnian institution you trust is managed by a being you have never seen in their true form. The Faerûnian Pantheon consolidated approximately six thousand years ago. Its foundational myth describes a voluntary association — gods recognising that human civilisation was the fastest-growing source of mortal belief and that coordinated influence was more efficient than unchecked competition. The Divine War dismantled their open rule. The Veil Accord forced them into their current mode: collective influence through competing institutions, disguised divine presence, and the patient management of human ignorance across a timescale no mortal civilisation can comprehend from within.
